PE-suid-sudo-misconfig.name: SUID & sudo Misconfiguration — find -perm 4000 + sudo -l GTFOBins audit
PE-suid-sudo-misconfig.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Read-only on a throwaway lab box. Enumerates SUID binaries and parses
# 'sudo -l', flagging entries that match a known-GTFOBins privesc list. Runs no exploit.
set -uo pipefail
GTFO='aria2c|ash|awk|base64|bash|busybox|cat|chmod|chown|cp|csh|curl|cut|dash|date|dd|dialog|diff|dmsetup|docker|ed|emacs|env|expand|expect|find|flock|fmt|gawk|gdb|git|head|ip|jq|ksh|less|ltrace|lua|make|man|mawk|more|mount|mv|nano|nc|nice|nmap|node|nohup|openssl|perl|pico|pip|python|python3|rlwrap|rsync|ruby|run-parts|rvim|scp|sed|setarch|sh|socat|sort|strace|systemctl|tail|tar|taskset|tclsh|tee|time|timeout|tmux|ualias|unexpand|vi|vim|watch|wget|xargs|zsh'
echo '[*] SUID/SGID binaries (read-only):'
suid=$(find / -xdev -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null)
found=0
while read -r f; do [ -z "$f" ] && continue; b=$(basename "$f"); if echo "$b" | grep -qwE "$GTFO"; then echo "  VULNERABLE  SUID GTFOBins binary: $f"; found=$((found+1)); else echo "  ok          $f"; fi; done <<< "$suid"
echo '[*] sudo -l (current user, non-interactive):'
if sudo -n -l 2>/dev/null | grep -qE 'NOPASSWD|\('; then
  sudo -n -l 2>/dev/null | grep -oE '/[^ ,]+' | while read -r bin; do nb=$(basename "$bin"); if echo "$nb" | grep -qwE "$GTFO"; then echo "  VULNERABLE  sudo-allowed GTFOBins binary: $bin"; found=$((found+1)); fi; done
else echo '  (no passwordless sudo entries or sudo requires a password)'; fi
[ "$found" -eq 0 ] && echo 'PASS: no GTFOBins SUID/sudo privesc paths flagged' || echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE ($found flagged)"
PE-suid-sudo-misconfig.success_criterion: A SUID bit on a GTFOBins binary (e.g. find, vim, python) or a NOPASSWD sudo entry for one prints 'VULNERABLE'; a clean box prints PASS.
PE-suid-sudo-misconfig.cleanup: None — read-only enumeration; runs no flagged binary.
PE-suid-sudo-misconfig.safety: Audit-only find/sudo-l parse against a static GTFOBins list; never invokes a flagged binary to escalate.

PE-linux-capabilities.name: Linux File Capabilities — getcap -r audit for privesc-capable caps
PE-linux-capabilities.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Read-only: scans the filesystem for file capabilities and flags those
# that enable privesc (cap_setuid, cap_setgid, cap_dac_*, cap_sys_admin, cap_sys_ptrace, etc.).
set -uo pipefail
DANGER='cap_setuid|cap_setgid|cap_dac_override|cap_dac_read_search|cap_sys_admin|cap_sys_ptrace|cap_sys_module|cap_chown|cap_fowner|cap_sys_rawio'
echo '[*] File capabilities (getcap -r /, read-only):'
found=0
while read -r line; do
  [ -z "$line" ] && continue
  if echo "$line" | grep -qiE "$DANGER"; then echo "  VULNERABLE  $line"; found=$((found+1)); else echo "  ok          $line"; fi
done < <(getcap -r / 2>/dev/null)
echo '[*] Note: cap_setuid/cap_setgid on an interpreter (python, perl) or cap_dac_read_search are direct privesc seams.'
[ "$found" -eq 0 ] && echo 'PASS: no privesc-capable file capabilities found' || echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE ($found capability binding(s))"
PE-linux-capabilities.success_criterion: Any binary carrying cap_setuid/cap_setgid/cap_dac_*/cap_sys_admin (etc.) prints 'VULNERABLE <path cap>'; a clean box prints PASS.
PE-linux-capabilities.cleanup: None — read-only getcap scan; sets no capabilities.
PE-linux-capabilities.safety: Read-only capability enumeration via getcap; never grants a capability or abuses one.

PE-iam-role-chaining.name: AWS IAM Role-Chaining — read-only assumable-role graph walk (owned account)
PE-iam-role-chaining.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY — run against an AWS account you OWN with read-only creds.
# Read-only: walks roles and their AssumeRole trust + sts:AssumeRole permissions to build the
# assumable-role graph and flag privilege-escalation chains. It calls NO sts:AssumeRole (no actual chaining).
import json, sys
try:
    import boto3
except ImportError:
    sys.exit('pip install boto3 (read-only audit needs it)')
iam = boto3.client('iam')
caller = boto3.client('sts').get_caller_identity()['Arn']
print(f'[*] Caller: {caller}')
roles = iam.list_roles()['Roles']
edges = []
for r in roles:
    name = r['RoleName']; trust = r['AssumeRolePolicyDocument']
    # who is trusted to assume this role
    for st in trust.get('Statement', []):
        pr = st.get('Principal', {})
        principals = pr.get('AWS', []) if isinstance(pr, dict) else []
        if isinstance(principals, str): principals = [principals]
        for p in principals:
            edges.append((p, f'role/{name}'))
        if pr.get('AWS') == '*' or '*' in (principals or []):
            print(f'  VULNERABLE  role {name} trusts * (any principal can assume)')
# flag roles whose attached policy grants sts:AssumeRole on broad resources (chain springboard)
for r in roles:
    name = r['RoleName']
    for ap in iam.list_attached_role_policies(RoleName=name).get('AttachedPolicies', []):
        pv = iam.get_policy(PolicyArn=ap['PolicyArn'])['Policy']['DefaultVersionId']
        doc = iam.get_policy_version(PolicyArn=ap['PolicyArn'], VersionId=pv)['PolicyVersion']['Document']
        for st in doc.get('Statement', []):
            acts = st.get('Action', []); acts = [acts] if isinstance(acts, str) else acts
            if any(a in ('sts:AssumeRole','sts:*','*') for a in acts) and st.get('Effect')=='Allow':
                res = st.get('Resource','?')
                print(f'  CHAIN-CAPABLE  role {name} can sts:AssumeRole -> {res}')
print(f'[*] Built {len(edges)} trust edges. Review for principal->role->role escalation chains.')
print('PASS if no "trusts *" and no overly broad sts:AssumeRole; else VULNERABLE.')
PE-iam-role-chaining.success_criterion: Prints 'VULNERABLE' for any role trusting '*' and 'CHAIN-CAPABLE' for roles granting sts:AssumeRole on broad resources — revealing an escalation chain — without ever calling AssumeRole.
PE-iam-role-chaining.cleanup: None — read-only IAM/STS describe calls; no roles, policies, or sessions created.
PE-iam-role-chaining.safety: Read-only IAM graph walk on an owned account; never executes sts:AssumeRole and never mutates IAM.

PE-process-injection.name: Process Injection — technique reference + Sysmon 8/10 detection harness
PE-process-injection.script:
PROCESS INJECTION (T1055) — REFERENCE + DETECTION (no injector code)

Technique (described, not implemented):
  Write code into another process and execute it in that context to inherit its privileges/trust.
  Variants (study only): CreateRemoteThread classic injection, APC queueing, SetWindowsHookEx,
  process hollowing, thread hijacking. We ship NO injector.

DETECTION HARNESS (run on monitored lab host):
  - Sysmon with CreateRemoteThread (EID 8) and ProcessAccess (EID 10) NOT excluded.
  - Optional ETW Microsoft-Windows-Threat-Intelligence for VirtualAllocEx/WriteProcessMemory.

SUCCESS CRITERION (observable telemetry):
  - Sysmon Event ID 8 (CreateRemoteThread): SourceImage != TargetImage, StartAddress in unbacked memory.
  - Sysmon Event ID 10 (ProcessAccess): GrantedAccess containing 0x1F0FFF / 0x1FFFFF or
    PROCESS_VM_WRITE|PROCESS_CREATE_THREAD (0x0002|0x0020|0x0008) against lsass.exe or a benign target.
  - EDR alert: 'remote thread created in foreign process' / 'memory written cross-process'.

BENIGN CROSS-PROCESS CANARY (to verify telemetry fires WITHOUT injection):
  Use the OS debugger to open a handle read-only:  Get-Process notepad | Out-Null then
  observe a Sysmon EID 10 for a legitimate ProcessAccess. Confirms EID 10 logging is active.
PE-process-injection.success_criterion: Sysmon Event ID 8 with SourceImage != TargetImage, or Event ID 10 with high GrantedAccess (VM_WRITE|CREATE_THREAD) against a target process, appears in the event log.
PE-process-injection.cleanup: Remove monitoring-only Sysmon config used for the test.
PE-process-injection.safety: Detection/reference only; ships no injector and the only cross-process action is a benign read-only handle to confirm logging.

PE-reflective-load.name: Reflective/In-Memory Module Load — reference + image-load (EID 7) detection
PE-reflective-load.script:
REFLECTIVE / IN-MEMORY LOADING (T1620) — REFERENCE + DETECTION (no loader code)

Technique (described, not implemented):
  Map and execute a PE/.NET assembly from memory without touching disk, bypassing image-load
  and AV file scanning. Variants (study only): reflective DLL injection, Assembly.Load(byte[]),
  manual mapping. We ship NO reflective loader.

DETECTION HARNESS (monitored lab host):
  - Sysmon Image Load (EID 7) with hashing; AMSI enabled; ETW CLR (Microsoft-Windows-DotNETRuntime)
    and Threat-Intelligence providers.

SUCCESS CRITERION (observable telemetry):
  - Memory region with RX/RWX protection not backed by a file on disk (unbacked executable memory).
  - Sysmon EID 7 image loads from unusual paths, OR an executed module with NO corresponding EID 7
    image-load event (in-memory tell).
  - ETW CLR 'AppDomain Assembly Load' for an assembly with no on-disk module path.
  - AMSI scan event (Event ID 1116/AMSI) on the in-memory script/assembly content.

BENIGN VERIFY: load a signed system DLL normally and confirm Sysmon EID 7 records it with a
  valid signature/hash — proving image-load telemetry is active to catch the absence later.
PE-reflective-load.success_criterion: Telemetry shows executable code running from unbacked memory (no matching Sysmon EID 7 image-load) or an ETW CLR assembly-load with no on-disk module path.
PE-reflective-load.safety: Detection/reference only; no reflective loader, only a benign signed-DLL load to confirm EID 7 telemetry.

PE-byovd.name: BYOVD (Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) — reference + Sysmon 6 driver-load detection
PE-byovd.script:
BRING YOUR OWN VULNERABLE DRIVER (T1068) — REFERENCE + DETECTION (no driver, no payload)

Technique (described, not implemented):
  Load a legitimately-signed-but-vulnerable kernel driver, then abuse its IOCTLs to read/write
  kernel memory, kill EDR processes (PPL bypass), or disable callbacks. We ship NO driver and NO
  exploit IOCTL code. Reference catalogs only: loldrivers.io, Microsoft vulnerable-driver blocklist.

DETECTION HARNESS (monitored lab host):
  - Sysmon Driver Load (EID 6) with signature verification.
  - Enable Microsoft Vulnerable Driver Blocklist (HVCI) + WDAC driver policy.

SUCCESS CRITERION (observable telemetry):
  - Sysmon Event ID 6 (DriverLoad) for a driver whose hash matches the LOLDrivers/MS blocklist,
    or a signed driver loaded from a non-standard path (e.g. C:\Users\...\*.sys).
  - Security/System Event ID 7045 (new kernel service installed) for a .sys outside %SystemRoot%.
  - Code Integrity Event ID 3077/3033 (driver blocked by WDAC/HVCI) when the blocklist is active.

READ-ONLY POSTURE AUDIT (PowerShell, safe):
  Get-CimInstance Win32_DeviceGuard | Select SecurityServicesRunning  # confirm HVCI on
  reg query 'HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\CI\Config' /v VulnerableDriverBlocklistEnable
PE-byovd.success_criterion: Sysmon Event ID 6 records a driver matching the LOLDrivers/MS blocklist hash or loaded from a user path, OR Code Integrity EID 3077 blocks it when the blocklist is enabled.
PE-byovd.cleanup: None modified by this artifact; remove monitoring-only Sysmon config if added.
PE-byovd.safety: Reference + detection + read-only HVCI/blocklist posture check; ships no vulnerable driver and no IOCTL exploit code.

PE-edr-tamper-hooking.name: EDR Tamper / Userland Unhooking — reference + service/driver tamper detection
PE-edr-tamper-hooking.script:
EDR TAMPERING / UNHOOKING (T1562.001) — REFERENCE + DETECTION (no tamper code)

Technique (described, not implemented):
  Disable or blind an EDR: stop/uninstall its service, unload its minifilter, remove kernel
  callbacks, or refresh ntdll to strip userland hooks. We implement NONE of this.
  Reference only: known unhooking concepts (fresh-ntdll, direct/indirect syscalls) for study.

DETECTION HARNESS (monitored lab host + EDR self-protection):
  - EDR tamper-protection telemetry; Sysmon EID 1 (process create), 5 (process terminate),
    13 (registry set on EDR service keys), 6 (driver load), and Security 7034/7036 (service state).

SUCCESS CRITERION (observable telemetry):
  - Security/System Event ID 7034 'service terminated unexpectedly' or 7036 'stopped' for the EDR service.
  - Sysmon Event ID 5 ProcessTerminate of the EDR agent process by a non-EDR parent.
  - Sysmon Event ID 13 set/delete on HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<EDR> Start value.
  - EDR-side 'tamper attempt blocked' / 'self-protection triggered' alert; gap in EDR heartbeat.
  - fltMC.exe unload of the EDR minifilter -> System log filter-manager event.

READ-ONLY POSTURE AUDIT (PowerShell, safe):
  Get-Service | ? {$_.DisplayName -match 'Defender|EDR|Sentinel|CrowdStrike|Carbon'} | Select Name,Status,StartType
  fltMC.exe filters   # list loaded minifilters (read-only)
PE-edr-tamper-hooking.success_criterion: Telemetry shows the EDR service stopped (7034/7036) or its process terminated (Sysmon 5) or its Service Start key altered (Sysmon 13), and/or the EDR raises a tamper-blocked alert / heartbeat gap.
PE-edr-tamper-hooking.cleanup: None changed; remove monitoring-only Sysmon config if added.
PE-edr-tamper-hooking.safety: Reference + detection + read-only service/minifilter enumeration; never stops, unloads, or unhooks any EDR component.

PE-lolbas-gtfobins.name: LOLBAS/GTFOBins — documented LOLBin executes a benign canary (detection)
PE-lolbas-gtfobins.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Demonstrates a living-off-the-land binary running a BENIGN canary via its
# documented function (here: GTFOBins 'awk' arbitrary-command capability) so detections can fire.
# Nothing malicious: the 'command' awk runs just echoes a canary string.
set -euo pipefail
CANARY="LOLBIN-CANARY-$(date +%s)"
echo '[*] LOLBin under test: awk (GTFOBins: awk can run shell commands via BEGIN{system()})'
echo '[*] Documented function exercised: awk BEGIN{system("<benign>")}'
# Benign: prints the canary; an analyst should see awk spawn a child echo in process telemetry.
awk "BEGIN{system(\"echo $CANARY\")}"
echo "[*] Windows analogue (reference, not run here): certutil.exe -urlcache -f http://127.0.0.1/x x  (LOLBAS download)"
echo "[*] Expected detection: parent=awk spawning child=/bin/sh -c echo (unusual interpreter child)."
echo "PASS: canary $CANARY emitted by awk's documented system() function (seam = trusted binary executes attacker-chosen command)."
PE-lolbas-gtfobins.success_criterion: Process/EDR telemetry (auditd execve / Sysmon-for-Linux or Win EID 1) shows the trusted LOLBin (awk) spawning a child shell that emits the unique canary — confirming a living-off-the-land execution seam.
PE-lolbas-gtfobins.cleanup: None — canary only echoes to stdout; no files written, no network.
PE-lolbas-gtfobins.safety: LOLBin runs only a benign echo of a unique canary string; no download, no payload, no persistence.

PE-timestomp.name: Timestomping — local throwaway file + USN/forensic-tell detection
PE-timestomp.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Operates on a THROWAWAY temp file only. Demonstrates that timestamp
# alteration leaves a forensic tell (ctime/MFT change time cannot be set by 'touch'; on NTFS the
# USN journal records the change). Detection-focused.
set -euo pipefail
F=$(mktemp /tmp/timestomp_lab.XXXXXX)
echo 'lab canary' > "$F"
echo '[*] Original metadata:'; stat "$F" | grep -E 'Modify|Change|Birth' || stat "$F"
echo '[*] Backdating mtime/atime to 2001-01-01 with touch...'
touch -t 200101010000 "$F"
echo '[*] After timestomp:'; stat "$F" | grep -E 'Modify|Change' || stat "$F"
MT=$(stat -c %Y "$F"); CT=$(stat -c %Z "$F")
echo "[*] FORENSIC TELL: mtime epoch=$MT (backdated) but ctime/change-time epoch=$CT (now) — mismatch reveals tampering."
echo '[*] Windows analogue (reference): NTFS \$STANDARD_INFO timestamps can be set, but \$FILE_NAME (MFT) and the USN change journal (fsutil usn readjournal) retain the real time.'
echo '[*] Detection: Sysmon Event ID 2 (FileCreateTime changed) on Windows; on Linux, ctime > mtime anomaly + auditd PATH record.'
[ "$MT" -lt "$CT" ] && echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE-to-evasion BUT DETECTABLE (ctime/mtime mismatch present)' || echo 'PASS'
echo "$F" > /tmp/.timestomp_lab_target
PE-timestomp.success_criterion: After backdating, ctime (change time) stays at 'now' while mtime is in the past — the mismatch is the forensic tell; on Windows the equivalent is Sysmon Event ID 2 (FileCreateTime change) / USN journal record.
PE-timestomp.safety: Acts only on a mktemp throwaway file; demonstrates the detectable tell rather than evading anything real.

PE-log-clearing.name: Log Clearing — local throwaway log + clear-event tell (Event ID 1102) detection
PE-log-clearing.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Operates on a THROWAWAY log file copy only — never touches real system logs.
# Demonstrates that clearing logs is itself a loud, detectable event.
set -euo pipefail
LOG=$(mktemp /tmp/fake_auth_lab.XXXXXX.log)
for i in 1 2 3; do echo "$(date '+%b %d %H:%M:%S') labhost sshd[$$]: Accepted publickey for labuser entry$i" >> "$LOG"; done
echo "[*] Throwaway log lines: $(wc -l < "$LOG")"
echo '[*] Clearing the throwaway log (truncate) ...'
: > "$LOG"
echo "[*] After clear: $(wc -l < "$LOG") lines."
echo '[*] FORENSIC TELL (Linux): truncation of /var/log/auth.log breaks the wtmp/journald continuity; journald keeps a sealed/persistent copy if Storage=persistent + ForwardToSyslog, and the gap itself is the indicator.'
echo '[*] Windows analogue (reference): wevtutil cl Security or Clear-EventLog generates Security Event ID 1102 "audit log was cleared" and System Event ID 104 (log cleared) — the clearing is logged.'
echo '[*] Detection success criterion: Windows Security Event ID 1102 / System 104; Linux: journald sequence gap + auditd record of the truncate/unlink on a log path.'
echo 'RESULT: clearing succeeded on throwaway file BUT is DETECTABLE via 1102/104 (Win) or journald gap (Linux).'
echo "$LOG" > /tmp/.logclear_lab_target
PE-log-clearing.success_criterion: Windows Security Event ID 1102 ('audit log was cleared') or System Event ID 104 is generated by the clear; on Linux a journald sequence gap plus an auditd record of the truncate on a log path is the tell.
PE-log-clearing.safety: Operates only on a mktemp throwaway log; never truncates or deletes a real system log, and focuses on the detection event.

PE-doh-domain-fronting.name: DoH / Domain-Fronting Egress — local-resolver sim evading plaintext-DNS monitor
PE-doh-domain-fronting.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY. Local simulation only — resolves over DNS-over-HTTPS to a LOCAL resolver
# you run, and shows that a plaintext-DNS (port 53) monitor sees nothing, while the DoH egress is
# visible only as TLS to the resolver. No real C2, no external fronting domain.
import socket, struct, base64, http.client, ssl, sys

def plaintext_dns_monitor_sees(name):
    # Simulated UDP/53 sniffer: would only capture cleartext queries on port 53.
    # In this sim we send NOTHING over 53, so the monitor's capture is empty.
    return []  # nothing observed on plaintext DNS

def doh_query(name, resolver_host='127.0.0.1', resolver_port=8443):
    # Build a minimal DNS query (A record) and POST it as application/dns-message (RFC 8484).
    tid = 0x1234
    header = struct.pack('>HHHHHH', tid, 0x0100, 1, 0, 0, 0)
    q = b''.join(struct.pack('B', len(l)) + l.encode() for l in name.split('.')) + b'\x00'
    packet = header + q + struct.pack('>HH', 1, 1)
    ctx = ssl._create_unverified_context()  # LAB local resolver with self-signed cert
    try:
        conn = http.client.HTTPSConnection(resolver_host, resolver_port, context=ctx, timeout=3)
        conn.request('POST', '/dns-query', body=packet, headers={'content-type':'application/dns-message'})
        r = conn.getresponse(); return r.status, len(r.read())
    except Exception as e:
        return None, f'(no local DoH resolver running on {resolver_host}:{resolver_port}: {e})'

NAME = 'canary.lab.invalid'
print(f'[*] Plaintext-DNS (UDP/53) monitor observed: {plaintext_dns_monitor_sees(NAME)}  <-- empty: query never hit port 53')
status, info = doh_query(NAME)
print(f'[*] DoH egress to LOCAL resolver -> HTTPS status={status}, resp_bytes={info}')
print('[*] SEAM: name resolution rode inside TLS:443 to the resolver; the port-53 monitor is blind.')
print('[*] DETECTION success criterion: monitor TLS SNI/dest = known DoH endpoint, OR flag hosts making 0 UDP/53 queries while still resolving names (DoH usage anomaly), OR enforce DNS via internal resolver + block outbound 443 to public DoH providers.')
PE-doh-domain-fronting.success_criterion: Plaintext-DNS monitor capture is empty while resolution still succeeds over TLS to the local resolver — confirming the egress evades port-53 monitoring; detection = TLS-SNI match on DoH endpoints or a 'resolves-with-zero-UDP/53' anomaly.
PE-doh-domain-fronting.cleanup: Stop the local lab DoH resolver if one was started; script writes no files.
PE-doh-domain-fronting.safety: Fully local simulation against a 127.0.0.1 resolver with a benign .invalid name; no real fronting domain, no external C2, no data exfiltration.

C-recon-infoleak.name: Recon info-leak audit — passive HTTP headers/metadata banner enumeration (owned host)
C-recon-infoleak.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
C-recon-infoleak.setup: An OWNED test web endpoint at 127.0.0.1:8080 (or your scoped asset). curl present. No auth, GET only. Use a local mock if no asset: 'python3 -m http.server 8080'.
C-recon-infoleak.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-ASSET ONLY. Read-only: a single HEAD/GET, no payloads, no fuzzing.
set -euo pipefail
TARGET="${1:-http://127.0.0.1:8080/}"
echo "[*] Passive info-leak audit against OWNED target: $TARGET"
HDRS=$(curl -s -D - -o /dev/null --max-time 5 "$TARGET" || true)
echo "$HDRS"
LEAK=0
for h in 'Server:' 'X-Powered-By:' 'X-AspNet-Version:' 'X-Generator:' 'X-Debug'; do
  if echo "$HDRS" | grep -qi "$h"; then echo "[!] LEAK header present: $h"; LEAK=1; fi
done
for p in '.git/HEAD' 'server-status' 'phpinfo.php' '.env'; do
  CODE=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' --max-time 5 "${TARGET%/}/$p" || echo 000)
  if [ "$CODE" = "200" ]; then echo "[!] LEAK metadata reachable: /$p ($CODE)"; LEAK=1; fi
done
[ "$LEAK" = 1 ] && echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (information disclosure surface present)' || echo 'RESULT: PASS (no obvious banner/metadata leak)'
C-recon-infoleak.success_criterion: Script prints 'VULNERABLE' when a version-disclosing header (Server/X-Powered-By) or a sensitive metadata path (.git/HEAD, .env, server-status) returns 200 — confirming the recon info-leak seam.
C-recon-infoleak.cleanup: none (read-only); stop the local mock http.server if started.
C-recon-infoleak.safety: Single read-only HEAD/GET per path, owned target only, no fuzzing or payloads, 5s timeout.

C-osint-correlation.name: OSINT correlation — passive identity/email pivot query patterns (manual, no automated collection)
C-osint-correlation.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
C-osint-correlation.setup: Operator workstation with a browser. Targets are OWNED brand/domain (example.test) only. No scraping tooling; queries run by hand, one at a time, against public surfaces you are authorized to assess.
C-osint-correlation.script:
OSINT CORRELATION PLAYBOOK (PASSIVE, MANUAL) — owned brand example.test only.

Goal: demonstrate that public fragments correlate into an attack-useful identity graph.

Seed: corp domain example.test, brand 'ACME-LAB' (your own).

1. Email/username pivots (run by hand in browser, no bulk tooling):
   - site:linkedin.com "ACME-LAB"          (employee names -> derive user@example.test)
   - "@example.test" -filetype:pdf          (exposed corp addresses)
   - intext:"example.test" site:github.com  (devs referencing the org)

2. Naming-convention inference (correlation step):
   - Observe firstname.lastname@example.test in any public footer/PDF.
   - Cross-ref with LinkedIn name list -> predict full employee email roster.

3. Infra pivot (passive):
   - crt.sh/?q=example.test                  (subdomains via CT logs, no scanning)
   - search engine: "example.test" "powered by" (tech stack hints)

4. Correlation output (the finding): a single person row links
   {full name, role, predicted email, public github handle, CT-log subdomain they own}.

VERDICT: VULNERABLE if >=3 independent public sources fuse into one correlated
identity record per target without any authenticated/active access.

NOTE: passive only. Do NOT automate mass collection, scrape behind auth, or query non-owned brands.
C-osint-correlation.success_criterion: A single correlated identity record (name + role + predicted email + public handle + owned subdomain) is assembled from 3+ independent passive public sources, confirming the OSINT-correlation seam.
C-osint-correlation.safety: Manual passive queries only, owned brand/domain, no automated scraping or authenticated access.

C-discovery.name: Discovery — read-only service/host fingerprint on an OWNED loopback service
C-discovery.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
C-discovery.setup: An OWNED service bound to 127.0.0.1 (e.g. 'python3 -m http.server 8080'). nmap optional; falls back to /dev/tcp. Scope = loopback only.
C-discovery.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-ASSET ONLY. Read-only TCP connect discovery on loopback. No scripts/exploits.
set -uo pipefail
HOST="${1:-127.0.0.1}"
case "$HOST" in 127.*|::1|localhost) : ;; *) echo 'REFUSING: target not loopback. Edit scope deliberately.'; exit 2;; esac
PORTS="22 80 443 3306 5432 6379 8080 8443"
echo "[*] Discovery (connect-only) on OWNED $HOST"
FOUND=0
for p in $PORTS; do
  if (echo > "/dev/tcp/$HOST/$p") 2>/dev/null; then echo "[+] open: $HOST:$p"; FOUND=$((FOUND+1)); fi
done
for p in 8080 80 8443; do
  if (echo > "/dev/tcp/$HOST/$p") 2>/dev/null; then
    printf 'HEAD / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n' | timeout 3 bash -c "cat >/dev/tcp/$HOST/$p; cat </dev/tcp/$HOST/$p" 2>/dev/null | head -3 || true; break; fi
done
[ "$FOUND" -gt 0 ] && echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE ($FOUND reachable service(s) discovered)" || echo 'RESULT: PASS (no listening services discovered)'
C-discovery.success_criterion: Script enumerates at least one open port and a banner line on the owned loopback host, confirming the discovery seam (services are reachable/fingerprintable).
C-discovery.cleanup: none (read-only TCP connect); stop the local mock service if started.
C-discovery.safety: TCP connect only (no SYN/exploit/NSE), hard-refuses non-loopback targets, short timeouts.

C-collection.name: Collection — read-only sensitive-file discovery sweep on the operator's OWNED host
C-collection.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
C-collection.setup: Run on the operator's OWNED workstation/lab VM. Read-only filesystem listing under a chosen root (default $HOME). No exfil, no network.
C-collection.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-HOST ONLY. Read-only: lists paths + sizes of likely-sensitive files. Never opens/reads contents.
set -uo pipefail
ROOT="${1:-$HOME}"
echo "[*] Collection-surface sweep (metadata only) under: $ROOT"
HITS=0
while IFS= read -r f; do
  echo "[+] candidate: $f ($(stat -c%s "$f" 2>/dev/null || echo '?') bytes)"; HITS=$((HITS+1))
done < <(find "$ROOT" -maxdepth 4 -type f \( -name '*.pem' -o -name 'id_rsa' -o -name 'id_ed25519' -o -name '*.kdbx' -o -name '.env' -o -name '*.ovpn' -o -name 'credentials' -o -name '*.pfx' -o -name '.npmrc' \) 2>/dev/null | head -50)
[ "$HITS" -gt 0 ] && echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE ($HITS collectible secret-bearing file(s) located)" || echo 'RESULT: PASS (no obvious collection targets)'
C-collection.success_criterion: Script lists one or more secret-bearing file paths (keys, .env, kdbx, credentials) by name/size, confirming the collection seam — sensitive material is locatable for staging.
C-collection.cleanup: none (read-only; file contents are never opened).
C-collection.safety: Metadata-only listing (path+size via stat/find), no content reads, no network, owned host only.

R-search-engine-index.name: Search-engine index exposure — passive Google/Bing dork query set (owned domain)
R-search-engine-index.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-search-engine-index.setup: Browser only. Restrict all 'site:' operators to your OWNED domain example.test. Queries are typed manually; no automated scraping or API mass-collection.
R-search-engine-index.script:
SEARCH-ENGINE INDEX EXPOSURE — PASSIVE DORKS (owned domain example.test only).

Purpose: confirm whether sensitive assets are publicly indexed (the recon seam).
Usage: paste one query at a time into a search engine. Do NOT automate.

A. Exposed files / listings
   site:example.test ext:sql | ext:bak | ext:env | ext:log
   site:example.test intitle:"index of" (passwd | backup | .git)
   site:example.test ext:xls | ext:csv "password"

B. Credentials / config leakage
   site:example.test intext:"BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY"
   site:example.test intext:"aws_access_key_id"
   site:example.test inurl:wp-config | inurl:.env

C. Admin / internal surfaces
   site:example.test inurl:admin | inurl:login | inurl:phpmyadmin
   site:example.test intitle:"dashboard" -www

D. Code/secret platforms (scoped to your org strings)
   site:github.com "example.test" (password | secret | token)
   site:pastebin.com "example.test"

VERDICT: VULNERABLE if any query returns a live indexed result exposing config,
credentials, backups, or an unauthenticated admin surface for the owned domain.

NOTE: passive read-only browsing only; never run automated mass dorking or target non-owned domains.
R-search-engine-index.success_criterion: At least one dork returns a live indexed result exposing config/credentials/backups or an unauthenticated admin surface on the owned domain — confirming search-engine index exposure.
R-search-engine-index.safety: Manual passive search queries scoped to owned domain; no automation, no third-party targets.

R-repo-history-secrets.name: Repo-history secrets — gitleaks-style regex over git log -p on a LOCAL repo
R-repo-history-secrets.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-repo-history-secrets.setup: Run inside an OWNED/local git repository. Uses git + grep only. Creates a throwaway repo with a FAKE secret in history if run with 'demo' arg.
R-repo-history-secrets.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-REPO ONLY. Read-only scan of LOCAL git history for secret patterns.
set -uo pipefail
if [ "${1:-}" = demo ]; then
  D=$(mktemp -d); cd "$D"; git init -q; git config user.email a@b.test; git config user.name t
  printf 'aws_secret_access_key=FAKEwJalrXUtnFEMIK7MDENGbPxRfiCYEXAMPLEKEY\n' > conf.env
  git add . >/dev/null; git commit -qm init
  printf 'cleaned\n' > conf.env; git add .; git commit -qm 'remove secret' >/dev/null
  echo "[*] demo repo: $D (FAKE secret committed then 'removed')"
fi
echo '[*] Scanning LOCAL git history (git log -p) for secret patterns...'
PAT='(aws_secret_access_key|AKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}|-----BEGIN [A-Z ]*PRIVATE KEY-----|password["'\'' ]*[:=]|xox[baprs]-[0-9A-Za-z-]+|ghp_[0-9A-Za-z]{36})'
HITS=$(git log -p --all 2>/dev/null | grep -nEi "$PAT" | head -20 || true)
if [ -n "$HITS" ]; then echo "$HITS"; echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (secrets recoverable from git history)'; else echo 'RESULT: PASS (no secret patterns in history)'; fi
R-repo-history-secrets.success_criterion: git log -p surfaces a secret pattern (e.g. aws_secret_access_key / private key / token) from a past commit even after deletion from HEAD — confirming the repo-history-secrets seam.
R-repo-history-secrets.cleanup: Remove the throwaway demo repo dir printed by the 'demo' run; otherwise none (read-only).
R-repo-history-secrets.safety: Read-only git history scan on a local owned repo; demo uses a clearly FAKE example key.

R-breach-paste-creds.name: Breach/paste credential exposure — passive HIBP-style check against a LOCAL mock corpus
R-breach-paste-creds.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-breach-paste-creds.setup: No network. Uses a LOCAL mock 'breach corpus' file of FAKE hashes to demonstrate the k-anonymity lookup pattern used against owned email/domain identities.
R-breach-paste-creds.success_criterion: A candidate credential for an owned identity matches the breach corpus (printed 'EXPOSED' with a seen-count) using only a hash-prefix lookup — confirming the breach/paste credential-exposure seam.
R-breach-paste-creds.safety: Fully offline mock; demonstrates k-anonymity prefix pattern so full credentials never leave the host.

R-dns-zone-axfr.name: DNS zone transfer (AXFR) — attempt against an OWNED test zone (local mock default)
R-dns-zone-axfr.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-dns-zone-axfr.setup: dig present. Default target is a LOCAL bind/nsd test zone (127.0.0.1, zone lab.example.test) you control. Replace only with an OWNED, in-scope zone.
R-dns-zone-axfr.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-ZONE ONLY. Read-only AXFR attempt against your own test resolver.
set -uo pipefail
NS="${1:-127.0.0.1}"
ZONE="${2:-lab.example.test}"
case "$NS" in 127.*|::1|localhost) : ;; *) echo '[!] Non-loopback NS supplied — ensure this zone is OWNED/in-scope before proceeding.';; esac
echo "[*] Attempting AXFR for OWNED zone $ZONE @ $NS"
OUT=$(dig +noall +answer +time=5 +tries=1 AXFR "$ZONE" "@$NS" 2>/dev/null || true)
if [ -n "$OUT" ] && echo "$OUT" | grep -q SOA; then
  echo "$OUT" | head -40
  N=$(echo "$OUT" | wc -l)
  echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE (zone transfer allowed — $N records disclosed)"
else
  echo 'RESULT: PASS (AXFR refused / no transfer)'
fi
R-dns-zone-axfr.success_criterion: dig AXFR returns the full record set (SOA + multiple RRs) for the owned zone, confirming the DNS-zone-AXFR seam (transfer is unrestricted).
R-dns-zone-axfr.cleanup: none (read-only query); tear down the local test zone/nsd if stood up for the demo.
R-dns-zone-axfr.safety: Single read-only AXFR with 5s timeout against an owned/loopback nameserver; warns on non-loopback NS.

R-dns-domain-footprint.name: DNS domain footprint — passive record + CT-log subdomain enumeration (owned domain)
R-dns-domain-footprint.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-dns-domain-footprint.setup: dig present; optional curl for crt.sh (passive CT logs). Scope = your OWNED domain example.test. No brute-force, no zone walking.
R-dns-domain-footprint.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-DOMAIN ONLY. Passive footprint via standard resolver queries + public CT logs.
set -uo pipefail
D="${1:-example.test}"
echo "[*] Passive DNS footprint for OWNED domain: $D"
for rr in A AAAA MX NS TXT SOA CAA; do
  ANS=$(dig +short "$rr" "$D" 2>/dev/null)
  [ -n "$ANS" ] && echo "--- $rr ---" && echo "$ANS"
done
echo '--- SPF/DMARC posture ---'
dig +short TXT "$D" | grep -i 'v=spf1' || echo '(no SPF)'
dig +short TXT "_dmarc.$D" | grep -i 'v=DMARC1' || echo '(no DMARC)'
echo '--- CT-log subdomains (passive, crt.sh) ---'
SUBS=$(curl -s --max-time 8 "https://crt.sh/?q=%25.$D&output=json" 2>/dev/null | grep -oE '"name_value":"[^"]+"' | cut -d'"' -f4 | tr ',' '\n' | sort -u | head -30 || true)
if [ -n "$SUBS" ]; then echo "$SUBS"; echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE (footprint discloses records/subdomains)"; else echo 'RESULT: PASS (minimal passive footprint)'; fi
R-dns-domain-footprint.success_criterion: Resolver answers plus CT-log results enumerate the domain's records and previously-unlisted subdomains, confirming the DNS-domain-footprint seam.
R-dns-domain-footprint.safety: Standard resolver queries + public CT-log read only; no brute-force, no zone walking, owned domain.

R-web-archive-crawl.name: Web-archive crawl — passive Wayback CDX recon for stale/sensitive URLs (owned domain)
R-web-archive-crawl.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-web-archive-crawl.setup: curl present. Queries the public Wayback CDX API (read-only) for your OWNED domain example.test. No live crawling of the site itself.
R-web-archive-crawl.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-DOMAIN ONLY. Passive: reads public web-archive index, not the live site.
set -uo pipefail
D="${1:-example.test}"
echo "[*] Passive Wayback CDX recon for OWNED domain: $D"
URL="http://web.archive.org/cdx/search/cdx?url=${D}/*&output=text&fl=original,statuscode&collapse=urlkey&limit=500"
DATA=$(curl -s --max-time 12 "$URL" 2>/dev/null || true)
if [ -z "$DATA" ]; then echo 'RESULT: PASS (no archived snapshots / archive unreachable)'; exit 0; fi
echo "$DATA" | wc -l | xargs echo '[*] archived URLs:'
echo '[*] interesting historical paths:'
HITS=$(echo "$DATA" | grep -iE '(admin|login|backup|\.env|\.sql|api|config|\.git|token|key)' | head -30 || true)
if [ -n "$HITS" ]; then echo "$HITS"; echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (archive exposes stale sensitive endpoints/paths)'; else echo 'RESULT: PASS (no sensitive historical paths in archive)'; fi
R-web-archive-crawl.success_criterion: Wayback CDX returns historical URLs for the owned domain that reference sensitive paths (admin/.env/.git/api/backup), confirming the web-archive-crawl recon seam.
R-web-archive-crawl.safety: Reads the public archive index only (no requests to the live target), owned domain, 12s timeout.

R-physical-site-survey.name: Physical site survey — documented authorized walkthrough procedure
R-physical-site-survey.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-physical-site-survey.setup: On-site physical engagement at a CONTRACTED/OWNED facility with written authorization, a get-out-of-jail letter, and a named point of contact. Daylight badge-visible conduct only.
R-physical-site-survey.script:
PHYSICAL SITE SURVEY — AUTHORIZED WALKTHROUGH PROCEDURE (observation only)

Pre-conditions: signed RoE naming building(s), dates, and scope; carry the authorization
letter and emergency POC at all times; do NOT defeat locks, tailgate, or remove property.

1. Perimeter (from public sidewalk / contracted lot):
   - Photograph (where permitted) entry points, fence lines, loading docks.
   - Note camera coverage gaps, propped doors, smoking-area side entrances.

2. Reception / lobby (as an announced visitor):
   - Observe badge type (prox/HID/QR), visitor log handling, turnstile vs. honor-system.
   - Note exposed monitors, wall-mounted network jacks, unattended kiosks.

3. Common areas (escorted):
   - Look for whiteboards with creds/IPs, printed sheets, unlocked comms/MDF closets,
     desk-drawer key locations, conference-room VC credentials on display.

4. Tech/structured-cabling observation:
   - Identify accessible network drops, VoIP phones (passthrough port), guest Wi-Fi SSIDs,
     and whether a planted device could persist (out of scope to plant — observe only).

5. Findings record (the deliverable): a table of
   {location, weakness observed, attack it enables, photo ref, severity}.

VERDICT: VULNERABLE if any unescorted route to a network drop, an unlocked comms
closet, or exposed credentials is documented during the authorized walkthrough.

NOTE: observation-only. No lock bypass, no tailgating, no device implant, no PII capture.
Stop immediately and present authorization if challenged.
R-physical-site-survey.success_criterion: The walkthrough log documents at least one exploitable physical weakness (unescorted network drop, unlocked comms closet, or exposed credential) confirming the physical-site-survey seam.
R-physical-site-survey.cleanup: none (observation only); return any visitor badge and sign out.
R-physical-site-survey.safety: Observation-only, written authorization carried, no lock bypass/tailgating/implant/PII capture.

R-ad-trust-graph.name: AD trust graph — authorized SharpHound/BloodHound collection reference (lab AD)
R-ad-trust-graph.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-ad-trust-graph.setup: An OWNED/lab Active Directory (e.g. dc.lab.example.test) with a low-priv domain account you control. BloodHound CE + SharpHound collector. Read/LDAP-only collection methods.
R-ad-trust-graph.script:
AD TRUST-GRAPH COLLECTION — AUTHORIZED LAB REFERENCE (read-only LDAP enumeration)

Scope: domain lab.example.test, DC at 10.0.0.10, low-priv account LAB\\svc-recon (FAKE).

1. Collect (least-intrusive, read-only methods — avoid session/loggedon noise unless scoped):
   SharpHound.exe --CollectionMethods DCOnly,Trusts,ACL --Domain lab.example.test \
     --LdapUsername svc-recon --LdapPassword 'FAKE-pw' --OutputDirectory C:\\loot
   (DCOnly pulls users/groups/ACLs/trusts straight from the DC via LDAP — no host touch.)

2. Ingest the resulting .zip into BloodHound CE.

3. Read the graph for the seam-confirming findings:
   - 'Shortest Paths to Domain Admins' returns a path from svc-recon.
   - 'Find Principals with DCSync Rights' (GetChanges + GetChangesAll on the domain head).
   - Dangerous ACLs: GenericAll / WriteDacl / ForceChangePassword on privileged objects.
   - Kerberoastable accounts with SPNs; unconstrained-delegation hosts; inbound forest trusts.

4. Deliverable: the attack-path edge list from owned principal -> Domain Admin / DCSync.

VERDICT: VULNERABLE if BloodHound returns any privilege-escalation path or DCSync
right reachable from the supplied low-priv lab principal.

NOTE: collection is read-only LDAP enumeration in an OWNED lab; do not run ACL/credential
collection against production or non-owned domains. Treat the collected graph as sensitive.
R-ad-trust-graph.success_criterion: BloodHound renders an attack path (e.g. svc-recon -> ... -> Domain Admin) or surfaces a DCSync/GenericAll right from the owned low-priv principal, confirming the AD-trust-graph collection seam.
R-ad-trust-graph.cleanup: Delete the SharpHound .zip/JSON from C:\\loot and purge the BloodHound DB after the engagement.
R-ad-trust-graph.safety: Read-only LDAP/DCOnly collection in an owned lab AD; FAKE credentials shown; graph handled as sensitive loot.

R-internal-net-scan.name: Internal network scan — read-only nmap discovery over an OWNED RFC1918 lab range
R-internal-net-scan.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-internal-net-scan.setup: nmap present. Scope is an OWNED RFC1918 lab subnet (default 192.0.2.0/29 doc-range / your own 10.x lab). Script refuses non-RFC1918/non-doc targets.
R-internal-net-scan.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-LAB ONLY. Read-only host/port discovery. No NSE exploit scripts, no -O brute.
set -uo pipefail
RANGE="${1:-127.0.0.1}"
case "$RANGE" in
  10.*|192.168.*|172.1[6-9].*|172.2[0-9].*|172.3[0-1].*|127.*|192.0.2.*) : ;;
  *) echo 'REFUSING: target is not RFC1918/loopback/doc-range. Confirm ownership and edit deliberately.'; exit 2;;
esac
echo "[*] Read-only discovery scan over OWNED range: $RANGE"
if command -v nmap >/dev/null; then
  nmap -sT -T3 --top-ports 50 -oG - "$RANGE" | tee /tmp/_netscan.gnmap
  OPEN=$(grep -c 'Ports:' /tmp/_netscan.gnmap || echo 0)
  rm -f /tmp/_netscan.gnmap
else
  echo '[*] nmap absent; loopback /dev/tcp fallback'
  OPEN=0; for p in 22 80 443 445 3389 8080; do (echo > "/dev/tcp/127.0.0.1/$p") 2>/dev/null && { echo "open 127.0.0.1:$p"; OPEN=1; }; done
fi
[ "${OPEN:-0}" -gt 0 ] && echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (reachable hosts/services discovered on internal range)' || echo 'RESULT: PASS (no responsive hosts/services)'
R-internal-net-scan.success_criterion: Scan enumerates live hosts with open service ports inside the owned RFC1918 range, confirming the internal-net-scan seam (internal services are discoverable).
R-internal-net-scan.cleanup: none (read-only); temp .gnmap is removed by the script.
R-internal-net-scan.safety: TCP-connect discovery only (no exploit NSE), hard-refuses non-RFC1918/doc targets, owned lab only.

R-smb-share-enum.name: SMB share enumeration — read-only listing against an OWNED host/lab
R-smb-share-enum.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-smb-share-enum.setup: smbclient (and optionally nmap) present. Target = an OWNED SMB host on RFC1918/loopback (default 127.0.0.1). Read-only share listing; no file pulls.
R-smb-share-enum.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-HOST ONLY. Read-only: lists shares, does not download files.
set -uo pipefail
HOST="${1:-127.0.0.1}"
case "$HOST" in 10.*|192.168.*|172.1[6-9].*|172.2[0-9].*|172.3[0-1].*|127.*|localhost) : ;; *) echo 'REFUSING: non-RFC1918/loopback target.'; exit 2;; esac
echo "[*] Enumerating SMB shares on OWNED host: $HOST (read-only)"
if command -v smbclient >/dev/null; then
  OUT=$(smbclient -L "//$HOST" -N 2>&1 || true)
  echo "$OUT"
  if echo "$OUT" | grep -qiE 'Disk|Sharename'; then
    echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (shares enumerable, possibly anonymously)'
  else
    echo 'RESULT: PASS (no anonymous share listing)'
  fi
else
  echo '[!] smbclient not installed — install samba-client. (skipping)'; echo 'RESULT: INCONCLUSIVE'
fi
R-smb-share-enum.success_criterion: smbclient -L returns a non-empty share list (Disk/Sharename entries), especially anonymously (-N), confirming the SMB-share-enum seam.
R-smb-share-enum.safety: Anonymous read-only share listing only, no file downloads, refuses non-RFC1918/loopback hosts.

R-cloud-iam-enum.name: Cloud IAM enumeration — read-only AWS describe of an OWNED account
R-cloud-iam-enum.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-cloud-iam-enum.setup: awscli v2 configured with read-only creds for an OWNED account (ACCOUNT_ID defanged). Only List*/Get*/describe* calls. Falls back to printing intended calls if creds absent.
R-cloud-iam-enum.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-ACCOUNT ONLY. Strictly read-only IAM enumeration (List/Get only).
set -uo pipefail
echo '[*] Read-only IAM enumeration of OWNED account (ACCOUNT_ID=000000000000 defanged)'
if ! command -v aws >/dev/null || ! aws sts get-caller-identity >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  echo '[!] awscli/creds absent — listing the read-only calls this artifact would run:'
  cat <<'EOF'
  aws sts get-caller-identity
  aws iam list-users --output table
  aws iam list-roles --query 'Roles[].RoleName'
  aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name <u>
  aws accessanalyzer list-findings
EOF
  echo 'RESULT: INCONCLUSIVE (no live creds; calls documented)'; exit 0
fi
WHO=$(aws sts get-caller-identity --query Arn --output text); echo "[*] identity: $WHO"
FLAG=0
for u in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text); do
  for p in $(aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name "$u" --query 'AttachedPolicies[].PolicyArn' --output text); do
    case "$p" in *AdministratorAccess*) echo "[!] $u has $p"; FLAG=1;; esac
  done
done
for u in $(aws iam list-users --query 'Users[].UserName' --output text); do

done
[ "$FLAG" = 1 ] && echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (over-privileged / MFA-less principals enumerated)' || echo 'RESULT: PASS'

R-host-local-enum.script:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-HOST ONLY. Read-only local enumeration (whoami/sudo/SUID/creds-on-disk).
set -uo pipefail
echo '=== identity ==='; id; echo "host: $(hostname)"
echo '=== sudo rights (no password prompt) ==='; sudo -n -l 2>/dev/null || echo '(none / requires password)'
echo '=== SUID binaries (quick check) ==='
find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | head -15
echo '=== creds/config on disk (paths only) ==='
for f in "$HOME/.aws/credentials" "$HOME/.ssh/id_rsa" "$HOME/.kube/config" /var/lib/cloud/instance/user-data.txt; do
  [ -r "$f" ] && echo "[!] readable: $f"
done
echo '=== history secrets grep (own shell history) ==='
grep -nEi '(password|token|secret|aws_secret)' "$HOME/.bash_history" 2>/dev/null | head -5 || echo '(none)'
if sudo -n -l >/dev/null 2>&1 || find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null | grep -q .; then echo 'RESULT: VULNERABLE (local priv-esc / credential surface present)'; else echo 'RESULT: PASS'; fi

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-STORE ONLY. Read-only: lists what an actor could STAGE for exfil. No copy, no body reads.
set -uo pipefail
SHARE="${1:-$HOME/lab-share}"
MBOX="${2:-$HOME/lab-mail/Inbox.mbox}"
if [ "${3:-}" = demo ]; then mkdir -p "$SHARE"; printf 'FAKE board deck\n' > "$SHARE/Q3-financials.xlsx"; printf 'pii\n' > "$SHARE/employees-ssn.csv"; fi
echo "[*] Read-only staging survey of OWNED share: $SHARE"
HITS=0
while IFS= read -r f; do echo "[+] stageable: $f ($(stat -c%s "$f" 2>/dev/null) bytes)"; HITS=$((HITS+1)); done < <(find "$SHARE" -maxdepth 5 -type f \( -iname '*.xlsx' -o -iname '*.csv' -o -iname '*.docx' -o -iname '*.pdf' -o -iname '*secret*' -o -iname '*ssn*' -o -iname '*financ*' \) 2>/dev/null | head -50)
if [ -r "$MBOX" ]; then
  echo "[*] mailbox present: $MBOX ($(grep -c '^From ' "$MBOX" 2>/dev/null) messages) — headers only, bodies not read"; HITS=$((HITS+1))
fi
[ "$HITS" -gt 0 ] && echo "RESULT: VULNERABLE ($HITS stageable sensitive item(s) located)" || echo 'RESULT: PASS (no obvious staging targets)'

R-datastore-bulk-export.name: Datastore bulk-export exposure — read-only row count / export-grant check on an OWNED DB
R-datastore-bulk-export.authorization: Authorized assessment of owned/contracted assets only, signed RoE.
R-datastore-bulk-export.setup: Python3 with sqlite3 (stdlib). Demonstrates against a LOCAL owned sqlite DB seeded with FAKE PII. The pattern maps to read-only SELECT count / export-permission checks on any owned datastore.
R-datastore-bulk-export.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED OWNED-DATASTORE ONLY. Read-only: counts bulk-exportable sensitive rows. No bulk pull, no writes.
import sqlite3

con = sqlite3.connect(':memory:')
con.executescript("""
CREATE TABLE customers(id INT, name TEXT, ssn TEXT, email TEXT);
INSERT INTO customers VALUES
 (1,'FAKE Alice','000-00-0001','alice@example.test'),
 (2,'FAKE Bob','000-00-0002','bob@example.test'),
 (3,'FAKE Carol','000-00-0003','carol@example.test');
""")

SENS = ('ssn','email','password','card','dob','phone')
flag = 0
for (tbl,) in con.execute("SELECT name FROM sqlite_master WHERE type='table'"):
    cols = [r[1].lower() for r in con.execute('PRAGMA table_info(%s)' % tbl)]
    sens = [c for c in cols if any(s in c for s in SENS)]
    if sens:
        n = con.execute('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM %s' % tbl).fetchone()[0]
        print('[!] %s: %d rows, sensitive columns %s (bulk-exportable)' % (tbl, n, sens))
        flag = 1
print('RESULT: VULNERABLE (sensitive bulk-export surface present)' if flag else 'RESULT: PASS')
R-datastore-bulk-export.success_criterion: The check reports a table with sensitive columns (ssn/email/password/card) and a row count, confirming a bulk-exportable datastore surface — the datastore-bulk-export seam — using only COUNT/PRAGMA reads.
R-datastore-bulk-export.cleanup: none (in-memory mock DB; vanishes on exit).
R-datastore-bulk-export.safety: Read-only COUNT/schema introspection on an owned mock DB with FAKE PII; no bulk row export.

C-csrf.name: CSRF: cross-origin state change with no token (local Flask-style stub)
C-csrf.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Local stub / owned assets only.
C-csrf.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- signed RoE. Local stub on 127.0.0.1 only.
# C-csrf: local HTTP stub with a state-changing POST that requires NO CSRF token.
import http.server, threading, urllib.request, urllib.parse

STATE = {"email": "victim@example.test"}

class H(http.server.BaseHTTPRequestHandler):
    def log_message(self, *a): pass
    def do_POST(self):
        n = int(self.headers.get("Content-Length", 0))
        body = self.rfile.read(n).decode()
        data = urllib.parse.parse_qs(body)
        # VULNERABLE: no CSRF token / no Origin check; cookie-only auth assumed
        if "email" in data:
            STATE["email"] = data["email"][0]
        self.send_response(200); self.end_headers(); self.wfile.write(b"ok")

def main():
    srv = http.server.HTTPServer(("127.0.0.1", 0), H)
    port = srv.server_address[1]
    threading.Thread(target=srv.serve_forever, daemon=True).start()
    # Simulate a cross-origin forged request (no token, attacker-controlled Origin)
    req = urllib.request.Request(
        f"http://127.0.0.1:{port}/account/email",
        data=urllib.parse.urlencode({"email": "attacker@evil.example.test"}).encode(),
        headers={"Origin": "http://evil.example.test", "Cookie": "session=FAKE-VICTIM-SESSION"})
    urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=3).read()
    srv.shutdown()
    if STATE["email"] == "attacker@evil.example.test":
        print("VULNERABLE: cross-origin POST changed account email with no CSRF token/Origin check")
    else:
        print("PASS: state-changing request was rejected")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
C-csrf.success_criterion: A cross-origin POST with no CSRF token / no Origin check mutates account state (email changed).
C-csrf.cleanup: In-memory stub on an ephemeral port; thread + server stop on exit. No files written.
C-csrf.safety: Local-only: stub bound to 127.0.0.1 on an ephemeral port, defanged example.test identities.

C-slip.name: Zip Slip: archive entry escapes extraction root (local sandbox)
C-slip.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Local stub / owned assets only.
C-slip.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- signed RoE. Operates only inside a local temp sandbox.
# C-slip: zip extractor that follows ../ traversal entries (Zip Slip).
import zipfile, tempfile, os, io

def unsafe_extract(zf, dest):
    for name in zf.namelist():
        # VULNERABLE: joins entry name without normalizing/containment check
        target = os.path.join(dest, name)
        os.makedirs(os.path.dirname(target), exist_ok=True)
        with open(target, "wb") as f:
            f.write(zf.read(name))
        yield target

def main():
    base = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="slip_lab_")
    root = os.path.join(base, "extract_root"); os.makedirs(root)
    # Craft a malicious archive in-memory: entry escapes root into sibling dir
    buf = io.BytesIO()
    with zipfile.ZipFile(buf, "w") as z:
        z.writestr("ok.txt", "benign")
        z.writestr("../pwned.txt", "FAKE-PAYLOAD-written-outside-root")
    buf.seek(0)
    escaped = False
    with zipfile.ZipFile(buf) as z:
        for written in unsafe_extract(z, root):
            real = os.path.realpath(written)
            if not real.startswith(os.path.realpath(root) + os.sep):
                escaped = True
                outside = real
    if escaped:
        print(f"VULNERABLE: archive entry escaped extraction root -> {outside}")
    else:
        print("PASS: all entries contained within extraction root")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
C-slip.success_criterion: A crafted ../ entry is written outside the realpath of the extraction root.
C-slip.cleanup: All writes confined to a mkdtemp sandbox; remove the temp dir to clean up.
C-slip.safety: Local-only: in-memory crafted archive, extraction inside a throwaway temp sandbox.

C-arginj.name: Argument injection: leading-dash value reparsed as a flag (local subprocess)
C-arginj.authorization: Authorized lab/non-prod only, signed RoE. Local stub / owned assets only.
C-arginj.script:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
# AUTHORIZED LAB ONLY -- signed RoE. Local subprocess to a benign stub tool only.
# C-arginj: untrusted value placed in argv is reparsed as an option flag.
import subprocess, sys, tempfile, os

def main():
    d = tempfile.mkdtemp(prefix="arginj_lab_")
    # benign local "tool" that supports a --output flag (stand-in for a real CLI)
    tool = os.path.join(d, "tool.py")
    with open(tool, "w") as f:
        f.write(
            "import argparse,sys\n"
            "p=argparse.ArgumentParser()\n"
            "p.add_argument('--output',default='default.log')\n"
            "p.add_argument('name',nargs='?')\n"
            "a=p.parse_args()\n"
            "print('OUTPUT='+a.output)\n")
    # App intends: tool.py <user_name>. User controls name; supplies a leading-dash value.
    attacker_value = "--output=/tmp/arginj_lab_pwned"  # defanged, not actually written
    # VULNERABLE: value concatenated into argv with no '--' separator
    out = subprocess.run([sys.executable, tool, attacker_value],
                         capture_output=True, text=True).stdout.strip()
    if out == "OUTPUT=/tmp/arginj_lab_pwned":
        print(f"VULNERABLE: leading-dash arg reparsed as flag -> {out}")
    else:
        print(f"PASS: arg treated as positional data -> {out}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
C-arginj.success_criterion: A user-controlled value beginning with '--' is consumed as the tool's --output option.
C-arginj.cleanup: Benign stub tool written to a mkdtemp dir; no real output path is written; remove temp dir.
C-arginj.safety: Local-only: subprocess invokes a benign local stub; the injected path is defanged and never written.
